subject: physics
problem: Question: A man of weight w_m is in an elevator of weight w_e. The elevator accelerates vertically upward with acceleration a and, at a certain instant, has upward speed V. The acceleration due to gravity is g. The man climbs a vertical ladder inside the elevator at speed v relative to the elevator. Define F as the man's apparent weight (the normal force exerted by the elevator on the man), v_t as the man's instantaneous speed relative to the ground, and P as the man's rate of expenditure of energy (power output). Take upward as positive. Quantities are measured in SI units: weights and forces in \unit{N}, speeds in \unit{m/s}, accelerations in \unit{m/s^2}, and power in \unit{W}.

subject: physics
problem: Question: An orbiting space station is observed to remain always vertically above the same point on Earth. Where on Earth is the observer located? Describe the orbit of the space station as completely as possible, and determine the orbital radius for such an orbit. Use the following symbols and definitions for the solution: R is the orbital radius measured from Earth's center, R_0 is Earth's radius, M is Earth's mass, m is the mass of the space station, G is the universal gravitational constant, g is the gravitational acceleration at Earth's surface, v is the orbital speed of the station, and T is the orbital period. For a geostationary orbit, take T=24 hours. For numerical evaluation, use R_0=6.37×10^6 m and g=9.8 m/s^2. In the final answer, report the orbital radius R.

subject: physics
problem: Question: In an amusement park there is a rotating horizontal disk. A child can sit on it at any radius (Fig. 1.1). As the disk begins to speed up, the child may slide off if the frictional force is insufficient. Let m be the mass of the child, μ the coefficient of static friction, ω the angular velocity, g the magnitude of gravitational acceleration, and R the radius at which the child sits. Given m = $50\unit{kg}$, μ = $0.4$, ω = $2\unit{rad/s}$, and take $g=9.8\unit{m/s^2}$. What is the maximum radius R where the child can sit and still remain on the disk?

subject: physics
problem: Question: A cord passes over a frictionless, massless pulley. A mass m_1 = 9 \unit{kg} is tied on one end and a mass m_2 = 7 \unit{kg} is tied on the other end (Fig. 1.2). Let F denote the tension in the cord, g the acceleration due to gravity, and \ddot x the common magnitude of the linear acceleration of the masses, taken as positive downward on the m_1 side. Determine F and \ddot x. Use g = 9.8 \unit{m/s^2} for numerical values.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A brick of mass m is projected up a straight inclined plane with initial speed \dot{x}_0=5\unit{ft/s}. The plane makes an angle \theta=30^\circ with the horizontal. The coefficient of friction is \mu=\sqrt{3}/12 (assume the sliding and static coefficients are both equal to \mu). Take the gravitational acceleration as g=32\unit{ft/s^2}. Use a Cartesian coordinate x along the plane, positive up the incline. Time is t with t=0 at release. The overdot denotes time differentiation, so \dot{x}=\frac{dx}{dt} and \ddot{x}=\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}. Let t_1 be the time when the brick comes momentarily to rest on the way up (the turning time), let x_1 be the displacement along the plane from the initial position up to t_1, let t_2=\frac{1}{2}\unit{s} be the evaluation time, let \Delta t=t_2-t_1, let \Delta x be the displacement during the interval [t_1,t_2], and let S be the net displacement along x from the initial position at time t_2. Assume the brick slides without rolling and that friction always opposes the direction of motion. Find S.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A person of mass m jumps from a height h and lands without bending his knees. After contact, he decelerates over a stopping distance s. Let g denote the acceleration due to gravity, f the total force on his legs during deceleration, E_1 the mechanical energy just before contact, and E_2 the work done by the force f during the stopping distance. Given m = 80\unit{kg}, h = 1\unit{m}, and s = 1/100\unit{m}, calculate f.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Consider a uniformly rotating spherical planet of mass M and radius R. The linear speed of a point on its equator is V. Let g_p denote the gravitational acceleration magnitude at the surface at the pole, and let the effective gravitational acceleration magnitude at the equator be g_e. It is given that g_e is one half of g_p due to rotation. Let G be the universal gravitational constant. A test particle of mass m is launched from the planet's pole. Let v_{esc} denote the escape speed from the pole. Let E denote the total mechanical energy and U the gravitational potential energy measured relative to zero at infinity. Determine v_{esc} as a multiple of V.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A small mass m rests at the edge of a horizontal disk of radius R. The coefficient of static friction between the mass and the disk is μ. The magnitude of gravitational acceleration is g. The disk is rotated about its axis until the mass slides off the disk and lands on the floor a vertical distance h below the disk, where h is measured in \unit{m}. Let D denote the horizontal distance traveled from the point where the mass leaves the disk to the landing point on the floor. Find D.

subject: physics
problem:
Question: A marble bounces down identical stairs in a regular manner, hitting each step at the same place and reaching the same height above each step. Choose unit vectors i (horizontal along the tread) and j (vertical upward) as in Fig. 1.5. Each step has rise equal to tread, both equal to l. Let g be the gravitational acceleration and m the mass of the marble. Let the marble move with constant horizontal speed v_h. Denote by v_i and v_f the vertical components of the marble's velocity immediately before and after each impact, respectively, with the sign convention that j is positive upward. Denote by v_1 and v_2 the total velocity vectors just before and just after an impact, respectively. Let t be the flight time between successive impacts. Let H be the maximum height above a step reached after each impact. The coefficient of restitution e is given and is defined by
$$
 e = -\frac{v_f}{v_i}
$$
Find the necessary horizontal velocity v_h for this steady bouncing pattern and the bounce height H above a step reached after each impact.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Assume all surfaces are frictionless and the inertia of the pulley and cord are negligible (Fig. 1.6). Let M be the mass of a large block on a horizontal surface, with a small block of mass m_1 on its top connected by a massless cord passing over a frictionless pulley to a hanging mass m_2, as shown schematically in Fig. 1.7. A horizontal external force F is applied to M. Let x denote the common horizontal displacement of M and m_1 in an inertial frame, and let ddot x be their common horizontal acceleration when there is no relative motion among M, m_1, and m_2. Let f_1 be the tension in the cord, and let g be the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration with units \unit{m/s^2}. Find the magnitude of the horizontal force F required to prevent any relative motion among M, m_1, and m_2.

subject: physics
problem: Question: The Sun is at distance R = 25000 light years from the center of the galaxy and moves approximately in a circular orbit with period T = 170000000 years. The Earth is at distance r = 8 light minutes from the Sun and moves approximately in a circular orbit with period t = 1 year. Assume the galaxy's gravitational force on the Sun can be approximated by concentrating all galactic mass M at the galactic center. Let m be the mass of the Earth, m_s be the mass of the Sun, G be the gravitational constant, v be the Earth's orbital speed, and V be the Sun's orbital speed. Using only these data, find the approximate gravitational mass M of the galaxy in units of the Sun's mass m_s. Use the conversion 1 year = 525600 minutes, so 1 ly = 525600 light minutes.

subject: physics
problem: Question: An Olympic diver of mass m begins a descent from a diving board of height h=10\unit{m} with zero initial velocity. Take gravitational acceleration g=9.8\unit{m/s^2}. After entering the water, assume the buoyant force balances the diver's weight so the net gravitational-buoyant force is zero, and the only retarding force is a viscous drag F_d=b v^2 acting upward, where b is a positive constant and v is the downward speed. Let x denote the vertical depth below the water surface measured downward with x=0 at the surface. Let t denote time measured from the instant of impact with the water, so t=0 at x=0. Let T denote the elapsed time in air from the dive until impact. Let V_0 be the speed on impact with the water (at x=0). Let V(x) denote the speed at depth x under water, with V(x)=\frac{dx}{dt} for t>0.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A bicyclist experiences a total resistive force due to friction and air drag given by F = a V, where F is the magnitude of the resistive force, V is the bicyclist's speed, and a = 4 \unit{N s/m}. At maximum effort, the cyclist can supply mechanical power P = 600 \unit{W}. On level ground with no wind, the maximum steady speed v_max is the speed at which the propulsive force equals the resistive force. Determine v_max. Use F for force, V for speed, a for the proportionality constant, P for power, and v_max for the maximum steady speed.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A simple pendulum consists of a point mass m attached to a light inextensible string of length l, suspended from a fixed pivot. The pendulum is released from rest with the string initially horizontal. A fixed nail is placed a vertical distance d below the pivot along the same line as the pivot, so that when the string passes through the vertical position it catches on the nail and thereafter the mass moves in a circle of radius l - d about the nail. Neglect air resistance and assume the nail-string contact is frictionless and instantaneous. Let g denote the magnitude of the acceleration due to gravity with SI unit \unit{m/s^2}. Use SI units throughout: lengths in \unit{m} and speeds in \unit{m/s}. Find the minimum distance d_min, in terms of l, such that the mass just manages to complete a full circle about the nail after the string catches. In the solution, v denotes the speed of the mass immediately before and after the string catches on the nail, and v_1 denotes the speed at the top of the circular path about the nail.

subject: physics
problem:
Question: A car of weight W = 5000 \unit{lb wt} travels at a steady speed of V_0 = 60 \unit{mph} on a level road. It is then shifted into neutral at t = 0 and allowed to coast. The speed as a function of time t (in seconds) is given by the coasting law below. Find the horsepower P_{req} required to maintain a steady speed of V_{req} = 30 \unit{mph} on the same road. Assume the resistive force inferred from the coasting data equals the steady resistive force at the same speed, and that at steady speed the propulsive force equals this resistance. Let m be the car's mass related to its weight by W = m g. Let F(V) denote the magnitude of the resistive force as a function of speed V, and assume at constant speed the engine propulsive force equals F(V). Useful constants and unit relations: g = 22 \unit{mph/s}, 1 \unit{H.P.} = 550 \unit{ft.lb wt/s}, 60 \unit{mph} = 88 \unit{ft/s}. The coasting law is
$$
V(t) = \frac{60}{1 + t/60} \unit{mph}
$$

subject: physics
problem: Question: A child of mass m sits in a swing of negligible mass suspended by a rope of length l. Model the child as a point mass and the rope as massless and inextensible. Let theta(t) denote the angular displacement from the vertical, measured in radians, with theta>0 when the child is pulled back from the vertical. The father pulls the swing back to the initial angle theta(0)=1\unit{rad}, then pushes tangentially along the arc with a constant force of magnitude F=m g directed to decrease theta, until the rope becomes vertical at theta=0. Assume the small-angle approximation sin theta \approx theta is valid for theta<1. Let g denote the gravitational acceleration and define omega by omega^2=g/l. Find the duration of the push t_1, defined as the time elapsed from t=0 to the instant when theta(t_1)=0.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A particle of mass m moves under two forces: a central force f1 and a linear (Stokes) friction force f2. Let r(t) denote the position vector, r=|r| its magnitude, v=dr/dt the velocity vector, and t the time. The central force has magnitude f(r) and direction along the radial unit vector r̂, and the friction is proportional to the velocity with positive coefficient λ. The forces are defined by the relations below. The motion lies in a plane; adopt plane polar coordinates (r, θ) in that plane, where θ is the polar angle and r̂ is the radial unit vector. Let J(t) denote the angular momentum vector about the origin and let J0 be the initial angular momentum at t=0. Find J(t) for all t≥0.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Consider a uniformly rotating, self-gravitating spherical object of mass M, radius R, mean density rho, and angular frequency omega. Let m be a test mass at the equator with speed v, and let G be the gravitational constant. The condition to avoid centrifugal breakup is that gravity at the surface is at least as large as the required centripetal acceleration. Define the minimum density that prevents breakup as rho_min. For a given mass M, define the maximum allowed radius consistent with a density bound as R_max. For numerical estimates use G ≈ 6.7 × 10^{-11} \unit{m^3/(kg s^2)}. The Crab pulsar rotates at frequency f = 30 \unit{s^{-1}}, so omega = 2 pi f. In part b, take the pulsar mass to be about one solar mass, M ≈ M_sun ≈ 2 × 10^{30} \unit{kg} ≈ 3 × 10^{5} M_earth, where M_earth is the mass of the Earth. In part c, take the density of nuclear matter as rho_nuclear ≈ m_p /(4 pi R_0^3 / 3), where m_p is the proton mass and R_0 is a characteristic nucleon radius.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Two weightless rings A and B slide without friction on a smooth circular loop of wire whose axis lies in a horizontal plane, so the loop itself lies in a vertical plane. The highest point of the circle is H and the center is O. A smooth, weightless string passes through the rings A and B and carries three weights: one attached at the free end C, one attached at the free end D, and one attached at a point E between the rings. In equilibrium, the rings A and B are located at symmetric points that are each 30^{\circ} from the highest point H of the circle, as shown in Fig. 1.11. Let the magnitudes of the three weights be W_C, W_D, and W_E, respectively. Let T denote the common tension in string segments that meet at a smooth ring. Find the relation among W_C, W_D, and W_E.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Calculate the ratio of the mean densities of the Earth and the Sun using the following approximate data. Define rho_e as the mean density of the Earth and rho_s as the mean density of the Sun. Define the target quantity eta by eta = rho_e / rho_s. Use the following data: theta = angular diameter of the Sun seen from the Earth = 1/2 \unit{deg}, l = length of 1 \unit{deg} of latitude on the Earth's surface = 100 \unit{km}, t = one year = 3 \times 10^7 \unit{s}, g = 10 \unit{m/s^2}. Let r be the Earth–Sun distance, M_e and M_s be the masses of the Earth and the Sun, R_e and R_s be their radii, G be the gravitational constant, omega be the orbital angular speed of the Earth about the Sun with omega = 2\pi / t, and m be the mass of a small test body at the Earth's surface. Infer the Earth's radius R_e from l via the meridional relation 2\pi R_e = 360 l. Use the small-angle relation in radians for the Sun's angular diameter, theta = 2 R_s / r.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A satellite is in a stationary (geostationary) orbit directly above a point on Earth's equator. It transmits energy by a coherent microwave beam of wavelength lambda = 1\unit{m} from a circular mirror (transmitting aperture) of diameter D = 1\unit{km}. Use the following symbols: G for Newton's gravitational constant, M for Earth's mass, R for Earth's mean radius, h for the height of the stationary orbit above Earth's surface, m for the satellite mass, omega for Earth's spin angular velocity, T_E for Earth's rotation period, and D_g for the required linear size (diameter) of the ground receptor station. Unless otherwise indicated, use G = 6.67\times 10^{-11}\unit{N m^{2} kg^{-2}}, M = 5.98\times 10^{24}\unit{kg}, and R = 6.37\times 10^{4}\unit{km}.

subject: physics
problem: Question: An inclined plane of mass M rests on a rough horizontal floor. The coefficient of static friction between the plane and the floor is μ. A point mass m_1 hangs vertically and is attached to a light inextensible string that passes over a smooth fixed peg at the upper end of the incline; the other end of the string is attached to a point mass m_2 that slides without friction on the surface of the incline. The incline makes an angle θ with the horizontal. Let g denote the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration. Let T denote the tension in the string. Let a denote the common acceleration when the plane is at rest, taken positive downward for m_1 and positive up the incline for m_2. Let N be the normal reaction exerted by the floor on the plane, and let f be the friction force exerted by the floor on the plane, taken positive in the horizontal direction that opposes the net horizontal push on the plane. Let N_1 denote the normal reaction exerted by the plane on m_2. Define μ_min as the smallest value of μ for which the plane remains at rest relative to the floor.
answer: \mu_{\min} = \frac{m_2 \cos\theta \left(m_1 - m_2 \sin\theta\right)}{M (m_1 + m_2) + m_1 m_2 (1 + \sin\theta)^2 + (m_1 + m_2) m_2 \cos^2\theta}

subject: physics
problem: Question: Three point particles with masses m_1, m_2, and m_3 move in a plane and interact only through Newtonian gravitation. Let r_i be the position vector of mass m_i in an inertial frame, and let r_{ij}=r_i-r_j be the relative position vector with magnitude r_{ij}=|r_{ij}|. The gravitational constant is G. The total mass is M=m_1+m_2+m_3. When stated, the center of mass C is chosen as the origin, so that sum_{j=1}^3 m_j r_j=0. In part b, consider the special configuration in which the system rigidly rotates in its plane with constant and equal pairwise separations d. The corresponding angular frequency is denoted by omega. In part c, consider a uniformly rotating frame with angular velocity vector omega normal to the plane; the symbol × denotes the vector cross product. For part c grading, define the stability indicators S_parallel and S_perp, where S=1 means stable and S=0 means unstable; the final classification should be reported as equalities for S_parallel and S_perp.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Paris and London are connected by a straight subway tunnel that follows a chord of the Earth (see Fig. 1.19). A train travels between the two cities powered only by Earth's gravity. Assume the Earth is a stationary homogeneous sphere. Neglect friction. Given: tunnel length L=300\unit{km} and Earth's radius R=6400\unit{km}. Define variables and constants: G is Newton's gravitational constant, M is Earth's mass, m is the train's mass, g=GM/R^2 is the gravitational acceleration at Earth's surface, V is the gravitational potential energy measured relative to the Earth's surface, x in [0,L] is the coordinate along the tunnel measured from London (x=0 at London and x=L at Paris), r(x) is the distance from the Earth's center to the train, h is the perpendicular distance from the Earth's center to the tunnel, v(x) is the train speed, v_{max} is the maximum speed during the trip, T is the one-way travel time from London to Paris, and t is the substitution variable t=x/L used in the integration. Find v_{max} and T in SI units.

subject: physics
problem:
Question: Three fixed point sources are equally spaced about the circumference of a circle of diameter a centered at the origin (Fig. 1.21). Each source exerts on a point mass of mass m an attractive force of the form F_i = -k R_i, where R_i is the vector drawn from the ith source at position r_i to the point mass at position r(t). Let r_1, r_2, r_3 denote the position vectors of the three sources, and let r(t) and v(t) denote the particle's position and velocity. The total force on the mass is F(t) = 

$$v(t) = \frac{dr}{dt}$$

$$F(t) = \sum_{i=1}^3 F_i$$

The point mass is placed in the force field at time t = 0 with initial conditions

$$r(0) = r_0$$

$$v(0) = v_0$$

In part (c), we will use the magnitudes

$$r_{0m} = |r_0|$$

$$v_{0m} = |v_0|$$

and the condition

$$r_0 \cdot v_0 = 0$$

means the vectors are perpendicular.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A phonograph turntable in the xy-plane rotates at constant angular velocity ω about the origin. Let Ω = (0, 0, ω). A small body of mass m slides on the turntable. In the inertial lab frame, its position vector is r(t) = (x_lab(t), y_lab(t), 0). The local surface velocity of the turntable at position r is v(t) = Ω × r(t). The forces on the body in the lab frame are: an elastic restoring force of magnitude k |r| directed toward the origin, modeled as −k r, and a linear frictional force −c ( dr/dt − v ), where c > 0 is a constant. Attach a frame rotating with angular velocity Ω. In this rotating frame, let x(t), y(t) denote the body’s coordinates, and let dots denote time derivatives of these rotating-frame components. Define initial conditions x_0 = x(0), y_0 = y(0), \dot{x}_0 = \dot{x}(0), \dot{y}_0 = \dot{y}(0). Define i = \sqrt{-1} and z(t) = x(t) + i y(t), with \dot{z}_0 = \dot{x}_0 + i \dot{y}_0. Let x_\infty and y_\infty denote the limiting rotating-frame coordinates as t → ∞.
answer: y_\infty = y_0 - \frac{ m \left( 2 m \omega \dot{x}_0 - c \dot{y}_0 \right) }{ c^2 + 4 m^2 \omega^2 }

subject: physics
problem: Question: A nonlinear oscillator with mass m and coordinate x has potential energy given by $$U(x)=\frac{k x^2}{2}-\frac{m \lambda x^3}{3}$$ where k>0 is the stiffness constant and \lambda is a small coupling parameter. The equation of motion is $$m \frac{d^2 x}{d t^2}=-\frac{d U(x)}{d x}$$ Seek a solution to first order in \lambda using the perturbative expansion $$x_{(1)}=x_{(0)}+\lambda x_1$$ subject to the initial condition $$x(0)=0$$ Define $$\omega=\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$ For the zeroth-order motion use $$x_{(0)}=A \sin(\omega t+\varphi)$$ where A is an amplitude parameter and \varphi is a phase constant. In solving for x_1, you may use a particular integral of the form $$x_1^p=B+C \cos(2 \omega t)$$ with constants B and C, and a homogeneous part $$x_1^h=D_1 \sin(\omega t)+D_2 \cos(\omega t)$$ with constants D_1 and D_2. Define $$A'=A+\lambda D_1$$ Let the velocity be defined by $$v=\frac{d x}{d t}$$ Report the first-order solution x_{(1)}(t). Note that determining A' and A requires additional information such as the amplitude or the value of v(0).

subject: physics
problem: Question: A defective satellite of mass m_satellite = 950 kg is being towed by a spaceship in empty space. The two vessels are connected by a uniform rope of length L = 50 m with linear mass density mu = 1 kg/m. The spaceship accelerates in a straight line with constant acceleration a = 5 m/s^2. Definitions for use in the solutions: m_rope = mu L is the total mass of the rope. The coordinate x is measured along the rope from the satellite end toward the spaceship, with x = 0 at the satellite and x = L at the spaceship. The rope-segment mass from position x to the spaceship is m_rope(x) = mu (L - x). The symbol F denotes the force exerted by the spaceship on the rope. The symbol F(x) denotes the tension in the rope at position x. In part (c), at the instant of the mishap both bodies have the same speed v_0, and the spaceship’s acceleration changes to a constant deceleration of magnitude a_d = 1 m/s^2. Let t be the time elapsed after the mishap, and let Delta t be the time until the two vessels collide.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A simple pendulum consists of a bob of mass M suspended from a fixed pivot by a light, inextensible string of length L. It oscillates freely in a vertical plane with small amplitude. The string length is then very slowly shortened by a factor of 2 (adiabatically). By what factor does the small-angle amplitude of oscillations change? Use the following definitions in the solution: g is the gravitational acceleration, theta(t) is the angular displacement from the downward vertical, theta_0 is the (small) oscillation amplitude, phi_0 is the phase, omega is the small-angle angular frequency, T is the period, J is the action integral over one complete oscillation, P_theta is the canonical momentum conjugate to theta, N is the string tension, E is the oscillator's energy, Delta W is the work done on the oscillator due to the slow shortening, Delta E is the change in the oscillator's energy, Delta L is the (negative) change in length during shortening, L_i and L_f are the initial and final string lengths, theta_{0i} and theta_{0f} are the corresponding amplitudes, F is the amplitude multiplication factor defined by F = theta_{0f}/theta_{0i}, and C is a positive constant arising from the adiabatic invariant. Angle brackets <·> denote the time average over one period. Assume the small-angle approximation holds throughout, with |theta| << 1 so that cos theta ≈ 1 − theta^2/2, and that the change of L is slow enough for adiabatic invariance to apply.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A perfectly reflecting sphere of radius r and uniform density rho = 1 \unit{g/cm^3} is attracted to the Sun by gravity and repelled by sunlight reflecting off its surface. Assume the Sun is a point source and that the incident rays at the sphere are effectively parallel because R >> r. Define: I_s as the Sun's total luminosity, M_s as the Sun's mass, R as the Sun–sphere distance, c as the speed of light, G as the gravitational constant, h as Planck's constant, nu as photon frequency, I_nu as the Sun's luminosity per unit frequency, and N_nu as the photon number flux at frequency nu at distance R. Let theta denote the polar angle between the local surface normal and the incident ray direction. Let m be the mass of the sphere. Use cgs units. Given: I_s = 4 × 10^{33} \unit{erg/s} and M_s = 2 × 10^{33} \unit{g}. Report the final answer for r in \unit{cm}

subject: physics
problem: Question: A particle of mass m moves in the plane with Cartesian coordinates x(t) and y(t) given by x(t)=x_0 cos(\omega_1 t) and y(t)=y_0 sin(\omega_2 t), where x_0 and y_0 are constants with dimensions of length, and \omega_1 and \omega_2 are constants with dimensions of angular frequency. Let i and j denote the unit vectors along the x and y axes, respectively. Let r=x i+y j denote the position vector. Let F be the force vector acting on the particle with components F_x and F_y. Let V be the potential energy, T the kinetic energy, and E=T+V the total mechanical energy. Take V(0,0)=0 as the reference level.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A long-range rocket is fired from the surface of the Earth of radius R with initial velocity components v = (v_r, v_\theta) in plane polar coordinates centered on Earth's center. Neglect air friction and Earth's rotation, and use the exact Newtonian gravitational field of magnitude GM/r^2, where G is Newton's gravitational constant and M is Earth's mass. Let m be the rocket mass. Let H denote the maximum altitude (apogee height) above the surface, so the apogee radius is R + H. At apogee the radial component of velocity vanishes. Let v_\theta' denote the tangential speed at apogee. Derive an equation that determines H exactly from conservation laws. Then expand that equation to lowest order in H/R to obtain an explicit formula for H. Finally, verify that for purely vertical launch (v_\theta = 0 and v_r = v) the small-H/R result reduces to the familiar constant-g formula H = v^2/(2g), where g = GM/R^2.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A comet orbits the Sun. Its speed at aphelion is v_a = 10\unit{km/s}, and its speed at perihelion is v_p = 80\unit{km/s}. The Earth moves in a circular orbit of radius R = \frac{3}{2} \times 10^8\unit{km} with orbital speed v = 30\unit{km/s}. Let m be the mass of the Earth, m_s the mass of the Sun, m_c the mass of the comet, and G the gravitational constant. Let R_a be the comet's aphelion distance (Sun–comet distance at aphelion), and R_p its perihelion distance. Find R_a.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A classical particle of mass m with total energy E_0 and angular momentum L about point o enters a region with an attractive central potential V = -G(r) centered on point o. The motion is in plane polar coordinates (r, theta) about o, where r is the radial distance from o and theta is the polar angle shown in Fig. 1.32. Let t denote time. For convenience in part (a), define x = theta so that dx/dr = dtheta/dr. The particle is scattered by the potential. Define r_min as the distance of closest approach.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A comet of mass m moves toward the Sun from far away with initial speed v_0 relative to the Sun. The Sun has mass M and radius R. Let G be the gravitational constant. Take the Sun to be at rest and ignore all other bodies. Define the total collision cross section sigma as the area in impact-parameter space that leads to the comet striking the Sun. Let b denote the impact parameter (the initial perpendicular offset between the comet’s incoming asymptote and the Sun’s center), r the comet’s closest-approach distance to the Sun’s center (perihelion), and V the comet’s speed at r. Find sigma in terms of G, M, R, and v_0.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A particle of mass m moves in a plane under an attractive central force directed toward a fixed center O. Let r be the orbital radius for a circular orbit, θ the polar angle, and t the time, with overdots denoting time derivatives. The magnitude of the central force is f(r), depending only on r and acting radially inward. The angular momentum is conserved and defined by L = m r^2 \dot{\theta}. Question: Mariner 4 is to travel from Earth to Mars on a heliocentric Hohmann transfer ellipse with perihelion at Earth's orbit and aphelion at Mars's orbit. Assume coplanar, circular orbits for Earth and Mars with radii R_E and R_M, respectively. Neglect the gravitational effects of the planets on Mariner 4 outside their immediate vicinity. Use SI units: distances in \unit{m}, speeds in \unit{m/s}, time in \unit{s}, and the solar gravitational parameter in \unit{m^3/s^2}. Let G be the gravitational constant and M_S be the mass of the Sun. The Sun's standard gravitational parameter is defined by
$$\mu=G M_S$$
Define the circular speeds at the orbital radii of Earth and Mars by
$$v_E=\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{R_E}}$$
$$v_M=\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{R_M}}$$
Define the semimajor axis of the Hohmann transfer ellipse by
$$a_T=\frac{R_E+R_M}{2}$$
Define the transfer speeds at perihelion and aphelion by
$$v_p=\sqrt{\mu\left(\frac{2}{R_E}-\frac{1}{a_T}\right)}$$
$$v_a=\sqrt{\mu\left(\frac{2}{R_M}-\frac{1}{a_T}\right)}$$
Let u_{t,E} and u_{t,M} be unit vectors tangent to the circular orbits at R_E and R_M in the prograde direction. r_{min} \le b

problem: Question: Estimate the maximum radius R of a spherical asteroid from which a person could escape by a single vertical jump. Let the magnitudes be R_0 and V_0, respectively, and let phi be the angle between R0_vec and v0_vec, so that h = R_0 V_0 \sin phi at t = 0. For circular motion, let r_0 denote the constant orbital radius. For small radial deviations, write r = r_0 + x with |x| \ll r_0. The angular frequency of small radial oscillations is denoted by omega with units \unit{s^{-1}}, and the ordinary frequency is f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} with units \unit{Hz}.

subject: physics
problem: Question: We consider one-dimensional oscillators. Let x(t) be the displacement, m the mass, ω0 the natural angular frequency, γ the linear damping coefficient, A the amplitude of the external driving force, ω the driving angular frequency, and α a small parameter characterizing a weak anharmonicity. We use a complex displacement z(t) such that x(t) = Re z(t), where Re denotes the real part. The imaginary unit is i with i^2 = -1. A superscript * denotes complex conjugation. The complex driving force is F(t) = A e^{i ω t}, with the physical force given by Re F(t). The steady-state complex amplitude is z0, which we parameterize as z0 = B e^{-i φ}, defining the real amplitude B and phase φ. We define the time-average over one driving period T = 2π/ω by ⟨Q⟩ = (1/T) ∫_0^T Q dt. For part (b), we define B' = A/[m(ω0^2 − ω^2)]. The initial conditions are x(0) = 0 and ẋ(0) = 0. Additional constants used in intermediate steps are C, ψ, a, b, c, d, f, D, and θ, all determined below.
answer: x(t) = \frac{A \big( \cos(\omega t) - \cos(\omega_0 t) \big)}{m (\omega_0^2 - \omega^2)} + \frac{10 \alpha A^2 \cos(\omega_0 t)}{3 m^3 \omega_0^2 (\omega^2 - 4 \omega_0^2) (\omega_0^2 - 4 \omega^2)} + \frac{\alpha A^2}{m^3 (\omega_0^2 - \omega^2)^2} \Big[ \frac{1}{\omega_0^2} + \frac{\cos(2 \omega t)}{2 (\omega_0^2 - 4 \omega^2)} - \frac{\cos(2 \omega_0 t)}{6 \omega_0^2} + \frac{\cos\big( (\omega_0 - \omega) t \big)}{\omega^2 - 2 \omega \omega_0} + \frac{\cos\big( (\omega_0 + \omega) t \big)}{\omega^2 + 2 \omega \omega_0} \Big]

subject: physics
problem:
Question: Assume a solid, non-rotating Earth of uniform mass density ρ and radius R. A straight tunnel through the Earth's center connects Buffalo at r = R to Olaffub at r = -R. A small test stone of mass m moves along the tunnel with coordinate r(t), where t is time. The gravitational constant is G. The stone's simple-harmonic angular frequency inside a uniform sphere is defined by
$$
\omega_0 = \sqrt{\frac{4 \pi G \rho}{3}}
$$
If the stone is released from rest at r = R, the time to appear at Olaffub is
$$
T_1 = \frac{\pi}{\omega_0}
$$
Now the stone is thrown inward from r = R with initial radial velocity
$$
\dot r(0) = - v_0
$$
Find the initial speed v_0 such that the arrival time at Olaffub equals
$$
T_2 = \frac{T_1}{2}
$$
For the subsequent solution, the motion will be written in the form
$$
r(t) = A \cos(\omega_0 t + \varphi)
$$
where A is the amplitude and \varphi is the phase determined by the initial conditions at t = 0. The period of the motion will be denoted by T, and the arrival time in the thrown case will be denoted by t_a. Use SI units: R and r in \unit{m}, t, T_1, T_2, t_a in \unit{s}, v_0 in \unit{m/s}, and \omega_0 in \unit{s^{-1}}.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A particle of mass m moves along the x-axis under a conservative force with potential energy V(x) = c x / (x^2 + a^2), where c > 0 and a > 0 are constants. Let F(x) denote the force, defined by F(x) = - dV/dx. Let t denote time, and use dx/dt for the velocity. Let E denote the total mechanical energy, E = (1/2) m (dx/dt)^2 + V(x). In part (a), let x_1 and x_2 denote equilibrium positions found from dV/dx = 0, and use the second-derivative test to classify stability. For small oscillations about the stable equilibrium at x = -a, write x = -a + x' with |x'| << a, where x' is the small displacement. Let ω be the small-oscillation angular frequency and T the corresponding period, with T = 2π/ω. In part (b), the particle starts from the stable equilibrium position x = -a with initial speed v (the magnitude of the initial velocity). You must find the ranges of v that lead to (1) bounded oscillations, (2) escape to x → -∞, and (3) escape to x → +∞.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A 3-4-5 inclined plane is fixed to a rotating turntable. A block of mass m rests on the plane. The coefficient of static friction between the block and the plane is mu_s=1/4. The block is located at a radial distance r=40\unit{cm} from the center of rotation (see Fig.1.62). The plane makes an angle theta with the horizontal such that sin theta=3/5 and cos theta=4/5. Gravity has magnitude g=9.8\unit{m/s^2}. Let N be the normal reaction from the plane, f be the static friction magnitude, and P be the magnitude of the centrifugal force in the rotating frame. The centrifugal force is P=m omega^2 r, directed radially outward. At the threshold for impending downward motion toward the center, friction acts up the plane and satisfies f=mu_s N. Find the minimum angular velocity omega that keeps the block from sliding down the plane toward the center.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A mass m hangs vertically from a spring with force law F = -K(x - l), where x(t) is the instantaneous length of the spring measured downward from the moving upper support point P to the mass, K is the spring constant, and l is the relaxed length of the spring. Gravity acts downward with acceleration g. At t = 0, the support point P begins sinusoidal vertical motion relative to a fixed inertial frame with displacement A sin(ω t), where A is the amplitude and ω is the angular frequency; take downward as positive. Assume that at t = 0 the mass is at the static equilibrium position relative to P and at rest. Define ω_0^2 = K/m and introduce the shifted coordinate y(t) = x(t) - l - mg/K for convenience. Set up and solve the equation of motion for x(t).

subject: physics
problem: Question: A block of mass m slides without friction on an inclined plane (wedge) of mass M and fixed angle α. The wedge can slide without friction on a horizontal table (see Fig. 1.65). Let X(t) be the horizontal coordinate of the wedge in an inertial laboratory frame, positive to the right. Attach a coordinate frame to the wedge: axis x along the plane, positive up the incline, and axis y normal to the plane, positive away from the surface. Let x(t) be the coordinate of the block along the incline measured in this wedge-attached frame. Let N(t) be the normal force exerted by the plane on the block. Let g be the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration, directed downward, with SI units \unit{m/s^2}. Write sufficient equations to determine X(t), x(t), and N(t). Do not solve these equations.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A merry-go-round (carousel) starts from rest and rotates about a vertical axis with a constant angular acceleration of 0.02 revolution per second squared. A woman sits on a chair at radius 6 \unit{m} from the axis and holds a ball of mass 2 \unit{kg}. Find the force she must exert to hold the ball 5 \unit{s} after the motion begins. Report the vector holding force f and its magnitude f_{mag}, and specify the direction with respect to the chair's radius by giving radial, tangential, and vertical components. Use two frames with a common origin: L is an inertial laboratory frame and R is a frame rigidly attached to the carousel. Let \vec A denote a generic vector. Let \vec r be the ball's position vector, with magnitude r. Let \vec v' and \vec a' be the velocity and acceleration of the ball measured in R, respectively. Let \vec \omega be the angular velocity vector and \dot{\vec \omega} be the angular acceleration vector. Let g be the magnitude of gravitational acceleration. Let \vec F be the resultant of the holding force \vec f and the gravitational force on the ball, with weight equal to - m g \hat k. Use a right-handed orthonormal basis with \hat i radial outward from the axis toward the chair, \hat j tangential in the direction of rotation, and \hat k vertically upward along the axis of rotation.

subject: physics
problem:
Question: A planet of uniform density spins about a fixed axis with angular velocity \(\omega\). Its equatorial radius \(R_E\) is slightly larger than its polar radius \(R_P\). Define the small flattening parameter by \(\varepsilon=(R_E-R_P)/R_E\). The contribution to the gravitational potential resulting from this distortion is given by the quadrupole term
$$
\Phi(R,\theta)=\frac{2 G M_e \varepsilon R_E^2 P_2(\cos\theta)}{5 R^3}
$$
where \(R\) is the distance from the center, \(\theta\) is the polar angle measured from the rotation axis, \(M_e\) is the planet mass, \(G\) is the gravitational constant, and
$$
P_2(\cos\theta)=\frac{3 \cos^2\theta}{2}-\frac{1}{2}
$$
State a reasonable condition for equilibrium of the planet's surface and compute \(\varepsilon\) in terms of the dimensionless parameter
$$
\lambda=\frac{\omega^2 R_E}{g}
$$
where the surface gravitational acceleration is
$$
 g=\frac{G M_e}{R_E^2}
$$
Make a numerical estimate of \(\varepsilon\) for Earth using
$$
R_E=6378\times 10^3 \unit{m}
$$
$$
\omega=\frac{2\pi}{24\times 3600} \unit{rad/s}
$$
$$
 g=9.8 \unit{m/s^2}
$$
Use the convention that \(\hat i\) and \(\hat k\) denote the unit vectors along the \(x\)- and \(z\)-axes, respectively. Let \(\Delta m\) denote a surface mass element, and let \(\tau\) denote the unit tangent to the surface in the meridional plane. The final answer should be written in the form \(\varepsilon=\dots\) as requested.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A satellite of mass m' moves in a circular orbit of radius R around the Earth of mass M with constant angular velocity ω. The satellite is attitude-stabilized so that one side always faces Earth, i.e., the satellite-fixed frame rotates with angular velocity vector ω = ω e_z about the z-axis. Use a right-handed satellite-fixed coordinate system with origin at the satellite’s center of mass, e_y pointing toward Earth’s center, e_z along ω (normal to the orbital plane), and e_x completing the triad. Let R be the vector from the satellite’s center to Earth’s center with magnitude R = |R|. An astronaut inside takes a small object of mass m and lowers it a distance Δr toward Earth along the +y direction. At time t = 0, the object is released from rest in the satellite frame with x(0) = 0, y(0) = Δr, ẋ(0) = 0, ẏ(0) = 0, where r(t) = x(t) e_x + y(t) e_y is the object’s position relative to the satellite’s center. Let G be the gravitational constant. Let F denote the gravitational force exerted by Earth on the object. Assume the motion remains in the orbital plane so that z(t) = 0 for all t. Describe the subsequent motion as seen by the astronaut (in the satellite frame).

subject: physics
problem: Question: A perfectly smooth horizontal disk rotates with constant angular velocity ω about a vertical axis through its center O. A person standing on the disk at radial distance R from O pushes a perfectly smooth coin (negligible size) of mass m directly toward O, imparting to it an initial speed V relative to the disk. Consider the motion as observed in the rotating frame rigidly attached to the disk. Let a right-handed Cartesian frame (x, y, z) be fixed in the disk with the z-axis along the rotation axis, the x-axis chosen opposite to the initial velocity of the coin, and the y-axis completing the right-handed triad. Let r be the position vector and v the velocity in this rotating frame. Let k be the unit vector along +z. Let i denote the imaginary unit used later for a complex representation in the disk plane. Over a short time interval 0 ≤ t ≤ Ψ_t during which (ω t)^2 is negligible, show that the trajectory seen by the person on the disk is approximately a parabola and give its equation in the form y = f(x).

subject: physics
problem: Question: A body of mass m is dropped from rest at a height h above Earth's surface at latitude φ=40^{\circ} N. Neglect air resistance. Let g be the (constant) gravitational acceleration near Earth's surface, and let ω be the magnitude of Earth's angular velocity. Use a local Cartesian frame with origin at the point on the surface directly beneath the release point: x-axis pointing south, y-axis pointing east, z-axis pointing vertically up, with corresponding unit vectors i, j, k and position vector r=x i+y j+z k. The body is released at t=0 with initial conditions x(0)=0, y(0)=0, z(0)=h and \dot x(0)=\dot y(0)=\dot z(0)=0. For h=100 \unit{m}, calculate the eastward lateral displacement at impact due to the Coriolis force, and report it as the value of y at impact.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Consider the effect of Earth's rotation on a plumb-line and on a freely falling body at geographic latitude θ in the northern hemisphere. Let R be Earth's mean radius in \unit{m}, ω be Earth's angular speed in \unit{s^{-1}}, g be the magnitude of the apparent gravitational acceleration in \unit{m/s^2}, and g_0 be the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration for a non-rotating Earth in \unit{m/s^2}. Let m be the mass in \unit{kg} of the bob, and let L be the vertical distance in \unit{m} from the top to the bottom of the Sather Tower (Campanile), which is also the plumb-line length. Define α as the angle between the apparent gravity direction and the line pointing to Earth's center, and let F_e be the magnitude of the fictitious centrifugal force. For the free-fall part, let δ be the eastward horizontal displacement in \unit{m} of a body dropped from rest from height L. Neglect air resistance. Berkeley is at north latitude θ.

subject: physics
problem:
Question: Under especially favorable conditions, a well-isolated subsurface ocean current was observed to circulate counter-clockwise when viewed from directly overhead. The period of one full rotation of the current is T=14 hours. Assuming the circulation is governed by the horizontal Coriolis acceleration due to Earth's rotation, determine the geographic latitude theta and the hemisphere H in which the current was detected.

Use the following symbols and conventions for the solution:
- Adopt a local Earth-fixed coordinate system with x-axis pointing south, y-axis pointing east, and z-axis pointing vertically upward; the corresponding unit vectors are i, j, k.
- The current's horizontal velocity is v = v_x i + v_y j with speed v.
- The magnitude of Earth's angular velocity is omega = 2 pi / T_E, where T_E = 24 hours is the length of a mean solar day. The local rotation vector at latitude theta is omega = omega cos theta i + omega sin theta k.
- The Coriolis acceleration is a = -2 omega × v. Its horizontal component is a_H.
- The circulation is treated as uniform horizontal circular motion with angular speed Omega and radius r.
- Latitude theta is positive northward. The hemisphere indicator H takes values N (Northern) or S (Southern).

subject: physics
problem: Question: A small celestial object of diameter 1\unit{km} and uniform density is held together only by its self-gravity. It can be tidally disrupted by Earth if it orbits too close. Let O be Earth's center, C be the object's center, and l be the center-to-center orbital distance. Let M be Earth's mass, m be the object's mass, G be the gravitational constant, rho be the object's density, and omega be the orbital angular speed for a circular orbit. To test stability against tidal disruption, consider a unit mass located at a point on the line OC at coordinate x measured from C toward O, or at a general point with coordinates (x,y) relative to C. Angles theta and varphi are defined by the geometric relations given in the solution. Use M = 6\times 10^{27}\unit{g} and rho = 2\unit{g/cm^3}. Find the critical distance l (the Roche limit) below which the object is disrupted, and report l numerically in both cm and km.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A merry-go-round (MGR) has two orthogonal axes (x, y) painted on it and rotates in the Earth inertial frame (x_0, y_0, z_0) with constant angular speed ω about the vertical axis z_0. A bug of mass m crawls outward without slipping along the +x-axis of the MGR with constant speed v_0 relative to the rotating platform. Let g be the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration, directed along −z_0. Let t denote time. Define unit vectors e_x, e_y, e_z fixed in the rotating frame (x, y, z), and unit vectors i, j, k fixed in the Earth frame (x_0, y_0, z_0). The angular velocity vector of the platform is ω along +z_0. Assume that at t=0 the rotating frame (x, y, z) coincides with the Earth frame (x_0, y_0, z_0), and that the bug is at the origin at t=0. Use r = x e_x for the bug’s position in the rotating frame and v' = v_0 e_x for its velocity relative to the rotating frame. Report the total force F_b exerted by the MGR on the bug and give all components of F_b in the Earth-frame basis i, j, k as functions of time. Units: m in \unit{kg}, v_0 in \unit{m/s}, ω in \unit{rad/s}, g in \unit{m/s^2}, t in \unit{s}, and x in \unit{m}
answer: F_b = - m v_0 \omega [2 \sin(\omega t) + \omega t \cos(\omega t)] i + m v_0 \omega [2 \cos(\omega t) - \omega t \sin(\omega t)] j + m g k

subject: physics
problem: Question: Consider a collection of charged particles of masses m and charges e, all having the same charge-to-mass ratio e/m. Each particle moves under a conservative central force F(r) directed toward a common force center at the origin. A uniform magnetic field B is applied. Prove Larmor's theorem: the motion of the particles in a sufficiently small magnetic field B is identical to the motion in the absence of B when viewed in a coordinate system R that rotates with a suitably chosen constant angular velocity vector omega about the force center. Determine the appropriate omega and specify quantitatively what is meant by a “small” magnetic field. Work in SI units. Definitions and notation: L is the laboratory inertial frame. R is a frame with the same origin as L that rotates with angular velocity omega relative to L. The position vector of a particle is r, with magnitude r=|r|. Velocities and accelerations measured in L are v and a. Velocities and accelerations measured in R are v' and a'. The cross product is denoted by ×. The magnitudes of vectors use absolute value bars |·|. The goal is to find omega and to state the small-field condition as an inequality.

subject: physics
problem: Question: The pivot point of a rigid pendulum undergoes prescribed vertical oscillation. The vertical displacement of the pivot from its equilibrium position is given by eta(t), where the motion is harmonic with amplitude eta_0 and angular frequency omega. The pendulum consists of a massless rod of length L with a point mass m attached at its end. Let g denote the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration. Let theta(t) be the small angular displacement of the rod from the downward vertical, with theta measured positive toward the +x direction. Use a Cartesian frame with origin at the equilibrium pivot position o, x-axis horizontal to the right, and y-axis vertically downward. Let F denote the rod tension. The small-amplitude natural frequency is defined by omega_0 = sqrt(g/L). Let a denote a small initial angular amplitude parameter for the initial conditions described below.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A particle of mass m can slide without friction on the inside of a small tube bent into a circle of radius a that lies in a vertical plane. The tube rotates about its vertical diameter at a constant angular speed ω in \unit{rad/s}. Let g be the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration in \unit{m/s^2}. Use a frame rigidly attached to the tube, with origin at the circle center and orthonormal basis {e_r, e_θ, e_\varphi}, where e_r points from the center to the particle along the circle (in the plane), e_θ is tangential along the circle in the direction of increasing θ (in the plane), and e_\varphi = e_r \times e_θ is normal to the plane of the circle. Let θ(t) be the angular position of the particle measured from the top of the circle, with θ = 0 at the top, and let t be time in \unit{s}. Let Ω be the angular-velocity vector of the rotating frame with magnitude ω. Let N be the normal reaction of the tube on the particle. Let r be the position vector of the particle relative to the center, v' and a' be its velocity and acceleration relative to the rotating frame, respectively. Let F be the total applied force on the particle (gravity plus the normal reaction). Let v be the particle velocity as seen in an instantaneously coincident inertial frame, and let E be its kinetic energy. Tasks: 1) Write the differential equation of motion for θ(t). 2) The particle is slightly disturbed from the unstable equilibrium at θ = 0 and released from rest at t = 0. Find the angle θ_0 at which the kinetic energy E is maximized.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A cart of mass M carries a vertical pole with a ball of mass \mu suspended by a light inextensible string of length R attached at point P. The cart and ball initially move horizontally with speed V on a frictionless surface. The cart collides inelastically with a second cart of mass m and they stick together. Assume M \gg \mu and m \gg \mu, and neglect friction and air resistance. Let g be the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration. Show that the smallest initial speed V for which, immediately after the collision, the ball can complete a full vertical circle around point P is V = \frac{M+m}{m} \sqrt{5 g R}. In the solution, T denotes the string tension, V' is the common speed of the two carts just after the collision, and V_1 and V_2 are the speeds of the ball at the lowest and highest points of its circular path relative to the cart

subject: physics
problem: Question: A cart of mass m moves with speed v as it approaches a cart of mass 3m that is initially at rest. During the head-on collision a spring between them is compressed (Fig. 1.81). Assume the system is isolated with no external horizontal forces and the track is frictionless. Define u_max as the common speed of both carts at the instant of maximum spring compression; this is also the speed of the cart with mass 3m at that instant. Define v_1' and v_2' as the final velocities of the lighter and heavier carts, respectively, long after the collision. Define v_f as the common final velocity if the collision is completely inelastic; this equals the heavier cart's final velocity in that case.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Express all requested rotational periods in terms of G, L, and the masses. Assumptions and definitions: Newtonian gravity applies, orbits are circular due to symmetry, and non-gravitational effects are neglected. G is the gravitational constant. For a binary system, L is the separation between the two stars. For an equilateral triple system, L is the triangle side length. M1, M2, M3 are the stellar masses; in equal-mass cases, use M1 = M2 = M or M1 = M2 = M3 = M as specified. R denotes an orbital radius about the center of mass (CM). For binaries, l1 and l2 are the distances of M1 and M2 from the CM, respectively. v, v1, v2 are speeds in the CM frame. T, T1, T2, T3 are rotational periods. Unit vectors i and j denote the x and y directions where coordinates are used.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A particle of mass m, charge q, and initial velocity v along a line (lab frame) undergoes a head-on interaction with an identical particle (same m and q) initially at rest. Work in classical mechanics, neglect radiation, and use Gaussian units so that the Coulomb potential energy between two charges q separated by a distance r is U = q^2 / r. Define r as the distance of closest approach, define v' as the common speed of each particle at the instant of closest approach (lab frame), and define v_{f,inc} and v_{f,rest} as the final velocities (lab frame) of the incident particle and the particle initially at rest, respectively. Take the positive direction to be the initial direction of motion of the incident particle. Find r, v', v_{f,inc}, and v_{f,rest}, and justify your answers.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Two steel spheres are released from rest with their centers aligned on a vertical line above a steel plate, as shown in Fig. 1.85. The lower sphere has radius 2a and the upper sphere has radius a. The initial height of the center of the larger sphere above the plate is h. Let g denote the acceleration due to gravity. Assume all collisions (sphere–plate and sphere–sphere) are perfectly elastic, and assume the larger sphere collides with the plate and recoils before it collides with the smaller sphere. Let H denote the maximum height reached by the center of the upper (smaller) sphere above the steel plate. Find H.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A projectile of mass m is fired with initial speed V toward a target of mass M. The target contains a hole with a spring of force constant k mounted inside. The target is initially at rest and can slide without friction on a horizontal surface (see Fig. 1.87). Let V_e denote the common velocity of the projectile and the target at the instant of maximum compression of the spring. Define Δx as the maximum compression distance of the spring. Find Δx. Use SI units: m and M in \unit{kg}, V and V_e in \unit{m/s}, k in \unit{N/m}, and Δx in \unit{m}.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A heavy star of mass M and radius R moves with constant velocity vector U through a very dilute gas of mass density rho. The gravitational constant is G. The star gravitationally focuses the incoming gas and captures all atoms that strike its surface. Assume the thermal velocities of the atoms are negligible compared with |U| and that atom-atom interactions are negligible. In the star's rest frame, gas atoms approach from infinity with velocity vector -U. Let b be the largest impact parameter that still results in capture, v be the speed of a gas atom just before it strikes the stellar surface at radius R, and F be the drag force vector on the star due to accretion. Find F.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Consider a spherically symmetric, self-gravitating collection of identical point particles, each of mass m. Every particle moves on a circular orbit about the common center at radius r with the same kinetic energy T. The only interaction is Newtonian gravity with gravitational constant G. Let M(r) denote the total mass enclosed within radius r, v be the orbital speed at radius r, rho(r) be the mass density, and n(r) be the particle number density, all depending only on r. Find n(r) such that the density remains constant in time

subject: physics
problem: Question: Two stars of masses M and m, separated by a fixed distance d, move in circular orbits around their common center of mass, which is stationary in an inertial frame. The stars may be treated as point masses. The star of mass M undergoes an instantaneous, spherically symmetric supernova that ejects mass ΔM without imparting any impulse to the remnant and without directly affecting the other star. Immediately after the explosion, the separation is d and the velocities of the two stars are unchanged at that instant. Let G be the gravitational constant. Let r_1 and r_2 be the distances from the center of mass to the stars of masses M and m, respectively, before the explosion. Let ω be the common angular velocity of the initial circular motion. After the explosion, the remnant mass is M - ΔM. Define v as the speed of the new center of mass relative to the original inertial frame. Let T_0 = 1/2 (M - ΔM + m) v^2 be the kinetic energy associated with the translational motion of the new center of mass. Let V be the mutual gravitational potential energy of the two stars at separation d, and let T be their kinetic energy in the new center-of-mass frame. Let E = T + V be the total mechanical energy of the two-star system in the new center-of-mass frame immediately after the explosion. Show that the remaining binary system is bound when ΔM < (M + m)/2. Also note the physical constraint ΔM < M.

subject: physics
problem:
Question: Consider a very long one-dimensional chain of point beads connected by identical massless springs with spring constant K and equilibrium spacing a, as shown in Fig. 1.93. Each bead is constrained to oscillate along the x direction. All beads have mass m except for one bead of mass m_0 with m_0 < m located at site n = 0. Let x_n(t) denote the displacement of bead n from its equilibrium position along x. Let k denote the wave vector of a normal mode and ω its angular frequency. In all plane-wave ansatzes below, A, B, and C denote complex amplitudes, where A is the incident amplitude, B the reflected amplitude, and C the transmitted amplitude in scattering from the special bead at n = 0. Define the reflection probability R by
$$
R = \left| \frac{B}{A} \right|^2
$$
answer: R = \left\{ 1 + \left( \frac{m}{m - m_0} \right)^2 \left[ \frac{\sin( k a )}{1 - \cos( k a )} \right]^2 \right\}^{-1}

subject: physics
problem: Question: Three bodies of equal mass m, labeled by i=1,2,3, are constrained to perform small oscillations along three coplanar axes that intersect at a common point and form 120 degrees between each pair, as shown in Fig.1.94. Identical coupling springs hold these bodies near equilibrium positions located a distance l from the intersection along each axis. The equilibrium length of each spring is sqrt{3} l. Let t denote time. Let x_i(t) denote the displacement of body i from its equilibrium position along its axis, so that its distance from the intersection is x_i(t)+l. Each spring has spring constant eta. Let U denote the total potential energy. Let K and k denote the effective coefficients appearing in the equations of motion. Indices i and j each range over {1,2,3}. For small displacements, the extension of the spring connecting bodies i and j is denoted by Delta ell_{ij}. For normal-mode solutions we will use real amplitude a, real amplitudes b_i, real phases phi and phi', and real angular frequencies omega and omega'. We will also use real parameters c and d. The imaginary unit is denoted by iota

subject: physics
problem: Question: Three identical point masses A, B, and C, each of mass m, are connected in a line by two identical ideal springs of spring constant K and relaxed length a, as shown in Fig. 1.95. Motion is confined to one dimension with no damping. Let x_A(t), x_B(t), and x_C(t) denote the positions along an inertial axis chosen so that at t=0 the equilibrium positions are x_A(0)=0, x_B(0)=a, and x_C(0)=2a, and all masses are at rest. For t>0, mass A is subjected to a driving force F(t)=f cos(ω t); the other masses are unforced. Define the normal coordinates y_1=x_A+x_B+x_C, y_2=x_A−x_C, and y_3=x_A−2x_B+x_C. Define the normal angular frequencies ω_2=√(K/m) and ω_3=√(3K/m). Use SI units for all quantities. Calculate the motion x_C(t) of mass C.
answer: x_C(t) = 2 a + \frac{f}{3 m \omega^2} [ 1 - \cos(\omega t) ] + \frac{f}{2 m ( \omega_2^2 - \omega^2 )} [ \cos(\omega t) - \cos(\omega_2 t) ] + \frac{f}{6 m ( \omega_3^2 - \omega^2 )} [ \cos(\omega t) - \cos(\omega_3 t) ]

subject: physics
problem: Question: Consider a classical system of point masses m_i with position vectors r_i(t) in three-dimensional space. Each mass experiences a net applied force F_i(r_i,t). A dot over a symbol denotes a time derivative, and a centered dot denotes the Euclidean dot product between vectors. The total kinetic energy of the system is denoted by T. The time average of any time-dependent quantity A(t) over an interval of length τ is defined below. For periodic motion take τ to be the common period; otherwise consider the limit τ → ∞. In part (b), consider a single particle with position vector r(t), radial distance r = |r(t)|, potential energy V(r), and a constant C such that V(r) = C/r describes a central inverse-square law potential. The net force on the particle is F(t)

subject: physics
problem: Question: Three point masses m_1, m_2, and m_3 are located at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side length s and attract each other according to Newton's law of universal gravitation with gravitational constant G. Determine the rigid rotational motion that leaves all mutual separations unchanged. Use the Cartesian frame shown in Fig. 1.99 with unit vectors i and j along the x and y axes, respectively. Place the masses at coordinates (0,0), (s,0), and (\frac{s}{2}, \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} s). Let r_i denote the position vector of mass m_i, and let the center of mass be the point c with position vector r_0. Denote by f_{12} the force on m_1 due to m_2, by f_{13} the force on m_1 due to m_3, and by f_1 their resultant on m_1. Let R_1 be the radius of the circular orbit of m_1 about c, v_1 its speed, and T the common period of rotation.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Two non-relativistic particles of equal mass collide almost head-on as shown in Fig. 1.100. Let the particle masses be m_1 and m_2 with m_1 = m_2 = m. In the laboratory frame, particle 1 moves with velocity u i and particle 2 moves with velocity -u [ cos theta_0 i + sin theta_0 j ], where u > 0 is the common speed and theta_0 is the collision angle. The unit vectors i and j point along the x and y axes, respectively. Let r_1(t), r_2(t), and r_c(t) be the positions of particle 1, particle 2, and the center of mass, respectively, as functions of time t. The center of mass velocity is denoted by V, and primes denote quantities measured in the center of mass frame. Let E be the total kinetic energy in the laboratory frame and E' be the total kinetic energy in the center of mass frame. All velocities are expressed in \unit{m/s}, masses in \unit{kg}, and energies in \unit{J}. Derivatives with respect to time are written in the form \frac{d}{dt}.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A rocket is projected straight up and explodes into three equally massive fragments just as it reaches the top of its flight (Fig. 1.101). Neglect air resistance and assume a uniform gravitational field with constant acceleration g directed downward. The explosion occurs at height h above the ground. Choose a vertical axis with origin at the explosion point and positive direction downward, so the ground is at y = h. Immediately after the burst, the fragments have velocity vectors u_1, u_2, u_3, with vertical components u_{1y}, u_{2y}, u_{3y} (positive downward). The fragment masses are m_1, m_2, m_3 and they are equal. Fragment 1 moves straight down and reaches the ground after time t_1 measured from the burst. Fragments 2 and 3 both reach the ground after the same time t_2 measured from the burst. Let v_1 denote the positive downward vertical speed of fragment 1 immediately after the burst, so u_{1y} = v_1. Find the height in the form h(t_1,t_2)=..., where h(t_1,t_2) is the height of the fragmentation above the ground.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A satellite of mass m moves in a circular orbit of radius R with speed v about the Earth. The mass of the Earth is M and the gravitational constant is G. The satellite abruptly absorbs a small mass δm that was stationary relative to Earth before the collision. During the brief capture, neglect external torques and assume the new post-collision orbit is approximately circular. Let E and E' denote the total mechanical energy (kinetic plus gravitational potential) just before and just after the capture, respectively. Let v' be the immediate post-capture speed and R' the radius of the new circular orbit. Let the change in total energy be ΔE = E' - E. Find ΔE and R'.

subject: physics
problem: Question: For the system of two identical point masses and massless springs shown in Fig. 1.102, each mass has mass m. The left mass is attached to the left wall by a spring with constant K, the two masses are connected to each other by a spring with constant K', and the right mass is attached to the right wall by a spring with constant K. The masses are released from rest from the symmetric initial configuration shown, so that the displacements of the two masses from their respective equilibrium positions have equal magnitude and opposite sign throughout the motion by symmetry. Let x(t) denote the displacement of either mass from its equilibrium position under this symmetric motion, let ω be the angular frequency, and let T be the period. Calculate T.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Two point masses, each of mass m, are at rest on a frictionless horizontal surface. They are connected by a massless linear spring of natural (equilibrium) length l and spring constant \kappa. At time t=0, an impulse is applied to one of the masses in a direction perpendicular to the spring. Let \vec{I} be the impulse vector and let I be its magnitude. The spring always remains collinear with the line joining the masses (no bending). Define the following quantities used below: \vec{P} is the total linear momentum vector of the two-mass system, with magnitude P; E_k is the total kinetic energy; E_p is the spring potential energy; E is the total mechanical energy with E=E_k+E_p; \vec{v}_c is the center-of-mass velocity vector, with magnitude v_c; L is the magnitude of the total angular momentum about the center of mass; \varphi(t) is the azimuthal angle of the line joining the masses measured about the center of mass; x(t) is the instantaneous separation between the masses; l_M is the maximum separation attained during the motion; v_M is the maximum instantaneous speed achieved by either mass; v_{2,\max} is the maximum instantaneous speed of the second mass.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A uniform flexible chain with linear mass density μ hangs vertically from its upper end above a horizontal table. An external upward force F is applied at the upper end while the chain is being lowered onto the table, as shown in Fig. 1.103. Let h(t) denote the height of the free lower end of the chain above the table (equivalently, the length of the freely hanging portion). Let v(t) denote the downward velocity of the freely hanging portion, taking downward as the positive direction. Let g be the acceleration due to gravity. Assume that any portion of the chain that reaches the table comes to rest instantly and transfers its momentum to the table without rebound. Use SI units: μ in \unit{kg/m}, F in \unit{N}, g in \unit{m/s^2}, h in \unit{m}, v in \unit{m/s}, and t in \unit{s}. Find the equation of motion for h(t).

subject: physics
problem: Question: An ideal rocket starts at rest in free space with no external forces. Let t denote time with units \unit{s}. Let m denote the instantaneous mass of the rocket, and let m0 denote the rocket mass at the moment of ignition. Let v denote the rocket's velocity relative to an inertial frame, and let v0 denote the constant exhaust speed of the propellant relative to the rocket with units \unit{m/s}. Let P denote the rocket's linear momentum, with P equal to m times v. Let e denote the base of the natural logarithm. Using the ideal rocket equation, determine the residual rocket mass m (expressed in terms of m0) at which P is maximized. Report the final answer as m_maxP = ... in terms of m0.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A rocket is fired straight up from rest with initial velocity v(0)=0 and initial height h(0)=0. It expels mass at a constant rate and with constant exhaust speed u relative to the rocket, measured in SI units as u in \unit{m/s}. The mass flow rate is chosen so that the initial acceleration satisfies a(0)=0. Let m(t) be the rocket mass at time t with m(0)=m_0, where m is in \unit{kg} and t is in \unit{s}. Let g be the constant magnitude of the acceleration due to gravity acting downward, with g in \unit{m/s^2}. Take upward as the positive direction, and neglect air resistance. Let v(t) be the upward velocity and a(t)=\frac{dv}{dt} be the acceleration. Let h(t) be the height above the launch point, with h in \unit{m}. All results are valid while m(t)>0, i.e., for 0 \le t < \frac{u}{g}.
answer: h(t) = - \frac{1}{2} g t^2 + u \int_0^t \ln \frac{u}{u - g t'} dt'

subject: physics
problem: Question: A bucket of mass M (when empty) is pulled upward from a well by a rope exerting a steady upward force P. The bucket initially contains water of mass m. The water leaks out at a steady rate so that after a time T the bucket is empty. Assume the leaked water has zero velocity relative to the bucket at the moment it leaves, air resistance is negligible, and g denotes the magnitude of the acceleration due to gravity. The bucket starts from rest at t=0. Let v(t) be the upward velocity of the bucket at time t. Let M'(t) denote the total mass of the bucket and remaining water at time t. Use SI units: masses in \unit{kg}, force in \unit{N}, times in \unit{s}, velocities in \unit{m/s}, and g in \unit{m/s^2}. Find v(T), the velocity of the bucket at the instant it becomes empty.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A rocket with dry mass M_0 carries fuel of mass m_0 and takes off vertically in a uniform gravitational field of magnitude g, as shown in Fig. 1.105. Exhaust is ejected with constant speed U_0 relative to the rocket. Let t denote time from liftoff, V(t) the upward velocity, and M(t) the instantaneous total mass of the rocket plus remaining fuel. The fuel is completely ejected by time t_0, so M(t_0)=M_0. The upward direction is taken as positive.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A water droplet nucleates in a uniform, quiescent fog. As it falls under gravity, it sweeps up all fog lying in its path and retains all collected mass. Assume the droplet remains spherical, experiences no viscous drag, and buoyancy of the air is neglected. Let t denote time, R(t) the droplet radius, V(t) its downward velocity, and M(t) its mass. Let rho_1 be the density of the droplet, rho_2 be the density of the fog, and g the magnitude of gravitational acceleration with SI units \unit{m/s^2}. Define the density ratio eta=rho_1/rho_2. Overdots denote derivatives with respect to t. As t becomes large, assume the motion approaches uniform acceleration a with SI units \unit{m/s^2}, in the sense that V(t)=a t for large t. Find the asymptotic acceleration a.

subject: physics
problem: Question: An hourglass sits on a scale. The hourglass frame has mass M. The total mass of sand is m, and initially all the sand is in the upper reservoir. At time t=0, the sand is released and exits the upper reservoir at a constant mass flow rate dm/dt=λ until the upper reservoir is empty. Each grain falls freely through a vertical distance h from the upper outlet to the bottom of the lower reservoir, where it is collected. Neglect air resistance and take g to be the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration. Let W(t) denote the scale reading (the normal force exerted by the scale on the hourglass) measured in newtons \unit{N}. Define the following: V is the speed of a grain upon impact at the bottom, t1 is the free-fall time for a grain through height h, t2=m/λ is the time when the upper reservoir becomes empty, and t3=t2+t1 is the time when the last grains arrive at the bottom. For clarity, define W1, W2, W3, and W4 as the values of W(t) on Periods 1–4, respectively, where Period 1 is 0<t<t1, Period 2 is t1<t<t2, Period 3 is t2<t<t3, and Period 4 is t>t3. Draw and label quantitatively the graph of W(t) for all t>0, indicating the values on each time interval and labeling the times t1, t2, and t3 on the time axis.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A rocket moves in deep space, far from external forces such as gravity. The rocket carries a fixed onboard energy E. Let v denote the rocket's speed in an inertial frame, w the exhaust speed relative to the rocket (which may depend on the instantaneous total mass m of the rocket), m the instantaneous total mass of the rocket, m1 the initial mass at time t=0, and m2 the final mass after the propellant is exhausted. The rocket starts from rest at t=0. Let v_max denote the maximum speed achieved when m=m2.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A spherical dust particle falls through a water mist cloud of uniform density. The rate of accretion of mass onto the droplet is proportional to the volume of the mist cloud swept out by the droplet per unit time. The droplet starts from rest in the cloud. Let x(t) be the downward vertical position, with x=0 at t=0 at the initial position. Let R(t) be the droplet radius and M(t) the droplet mass. Let M0 and R0 be the initial mass and radius. Let v(t)=dx/dt be the velocity. Let g be the constant gravitational acceleration with SI units \unit{m/s^2}. Let ρ be the uniform mist density with SI units \unit{kg/m^3}. Let α>0 be the proportionality constant in the accretion law, with SI units \unit{kg/m^3}. Find the acceleration of the drop for large times. Define the final answer as a_large, the asymptotic value of d^2x/dt^2 for large t.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Suppose a spacecraft of initial mass m_0 in \unit{kg} and constant cross-sectional area A in \unit{m^2} is coasting with initial speed v_0 in \unit{m/s} when it encounters a stationary dust cloud of uniform mass density \rho in \unit{kg/m^3}, as shown in Fig. 1.108. The dust is stationary in the inertial frame before capture and sticks to the spacecraft upon contact (perfectly inelastic accretion). Assume the motion remains along the initial line of travel and that A is constant over time. Let m(t) in \unit{kg} be the instantaneous mass of the spacecraft including accreted dust, and let v(t) in \unit{m/s} be its speed. Time t in \unit{s} is measured from the instant the spacecraft first enters the dust cloud (t=0). Solve for the subsequent motion v(t) of the spacecraft.

problem: Question: A uniform cube has side length 2a. The origin is at the center of mass, and the Cartesian axes x, y, z pass through the centers of the three pairs of opposite faces.
