Let I_{xx}, I_{yy}, I_{zz} be the moments of inertia about the x, y, z axes through the center of mass, and let I_{xy}, I_{yz}, I_{zx} be the corresponding products of inertia. For an axis with direction cosines lambda, mu, nu relative to x, y, z, let I denote the moment of inertia about that axis. Let i, j, k denote the unit vectors along the x, y, z axes, respectively. Let r denote the radius vector from the origin to a chosen corner of the cube; in the solution we take the corner with positive x, y, z coordinates.

subject: physics
problem:
Question: A thin disk of radius R and mass M lies in the xy-plane with the z-axis pointing out of the page. A point mass m is attached at the rim of the disk, with m = 5M/4. Let A and B be pivots that constrain rotation about the y-axis as shown in the figures. Use a disk-fixed coordinate frame (x, y, z) attached to the disk and a laboratory frame (x', y', z') that shares the same y-axis as the disk-fixed frame and coincides with it at t = 0. Let ω denote the constant angular velocity about the y-axis, and let t denote time. Let L denote the angular momentum vector and I the moment of inertia tensor. The bare disk (without the point mass) has a moment of inertia tensor about its center of mass given by
$$
I_{\text{disk, CM}} = \frac{M R^{2}}{4} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$
The combined system will be described about point A unless otherwise stated. Define the rotation tensor S from the disk-fixed frame to the laboratory frame by the relations x' = x \cos(\omega t) + z \sin(\omega t), y' = y, z' = - x \sin(\omega t) + z \cos(\omega t). Vectors transform as v' = S v, and S is
$$
S = \begin{pmatrix} \cos(\omega t) & 0 & \sin(\omega t) \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ - \sin(\omega t) & 0 & \cos(\omega t) \end{pmatrix}
$$
When solving for principal moments about A, write I = \frac{M R^{2}}{4} \tilde I and let \gamma denote the eigenvalue parameter of the dimensionless matrix \tilde I. Unit vectors along the laboratory axes are denoted by e_{x'}, e_{y'}, e_{z'}.
answer: \vec F_{B}(t) = \frac{5 M R \omega^{2}}{8} \big( \cos(\omega t) \ e_{x'} - \sin(\omega t) \ e_{z'} \big)

subject: physics
problem: Question: Four point masses, each of mass m, lie in the xy-plane at positions (x, y) = (a, 0), (-a, 0), (0, 2a), (0, -2a). These are joined by massless rods to form a rigid body. Use the right-handed Cartesian axes x, y, z with origin at the intersection of the rods. Let I denote the inertia tensor about the origin with components I_{ij}. For a system of point masses indexed by n with masses m_n at positions (x_{n1}, x_{n2}, x_{n3}), the components are given by I_{ij} = \sum_n m_n (r_n^2 \delta_{ij} - x_{ni} x_{nj}), where r_n^2 = x_{n1}^2 + x_{n2}^2 + x_{n3}^2 and \delta_{ij} is the Kronecker delta. In this problem, m_n = m for all n. Let n be a unit vector with direction cosines \lambda, \mu, \nu relative to the x, y, z axes. Let I_n denote the scalar moment of inertia for rotation about the axis along n. Let \omega_{vec} denote the angular velocity vector and let \omega denote its magnitude, so that \omega_{vec} is parallel to n when specified. Let L denote the angular momentum vector about the origin. Let \varphi denote the angle between L and n, reported in degrees.

problem: Question: A flywheel in the form of a uniformly thick disk of diameter D=4\unit{ft} has weight W=600\unit{lbf} and rotates initially at 1200\unit{rpm}. Take g=32.2\unit{ft/s^2}. Compute \tau_{stop}.

problem: Question: A structure is made of equal-length straight members, numbered 1 through 11, hinged at joints A, B, C, D, E, F, G as shown in Fig. 1.112. Find the vertical support reactions N_{AY} and N_{GY}, and determine T_i or C_i for each member as appropriate

Figures 1.114–1.116 indicate the geometrical setup and force directions

problem: Question: A simple abstraction of a camshaft consists of two point masses m and 2m fixed on massless rods, all in a plane. subject: physics
problem: Question: A coin has its plane vertical and is spinning about a horizontal diameter (an axis in its plane through its center) with initial angular velocity ω. The coin is gently set down on a horizontal flat surface while remaining vertical. Neglect rolling friction. Definitions: N is the normal (supporting) force from the surface; P is the weight of the coin; m is the mass of the coin; g is the gravitational acceleration so that P equals m g; τ_ext is the external torque about the center of mass; L is the angular momentum of the coin about its center of mass; I is the moment of inertia of the coin about the same horizontal diameter; ω_f is the final angular velocity just after contact. Find ω_f.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Model the leg as a uniform rigid rod of length l and mass m that swings about the hip as a fixed pivot, neglecting the knee joint. Let g be the gravitational acceleration, θ(t) the small angular displacement from the vertical, I the moment of inertia about the hip, ω the angular frequency of small oscillations, and ν the oscillation frequency in hertz. Using the small-angle approximation, estimate the natural swing frequency ν that sets the comfortable walking cadence, and state which characteristic of the leg it depends on.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Cylinder c has mass M = 10\unit{kg} and radius R = \frac{7}{100}\unit{m}. It rolls without slipping on the incline H shown in Fig. 1.121. The string is inextensible and is wrapped around cylinder c. The other end of the string supports a hanging mass m = 2\unit{kg} that moves vertically. The incline angle is \alpha = 30\unit{deg}. Let x(t) be the displacement of the cylinder's center along the incline, positive upward, and \ddot{x} its acceleration along the incline. Let \theta(t) be the rotation angle of the cylinder, with the rolling constraint x = R \theta and \ddot{x} = R \ddot{\theta}. Let F be the string tension. Let f be the static friction force at the contact point P, defined positive upward along the incline. Let g be the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration. The cylinder's moment of inertia about its center is I = \frac{1}{2} M R^2. For part (a), let \Delta s_{mass} be the downward displacement of the hanging mass, \Delta x the displacement of the cylinder along the incline, and \Delta y_{vert} the vertical rise of the cylinder's center.

subject: physics
problem:
Question: A uniform hoop of mass M and radius R hangs in a vertical plane supported by a knife edge at one point on the inside circumference. Let g be the gravitational acceleration in \unit{m/s^2}. Let I be the moment of inertia of the hoop about the knife-edge support. Let t be time in \unit{s} and let 	heta(t) be the small angular displacement from the stable equilibrium position. Let \omega be the angular frequency of small oscillations and let 
u be the natural frequency defined by 
u=\omega/(2\pi). Calculate the natural frequency 
u of small oscillations.

subject: physics
problem: Question: An ultra-high speed rotor consists of a homogeneous disc of mass M, radius R, and axial half-width l (so the total width is 2l). It is mounted on a shaft supported on bearings separated by a distance 2d along the shaft, as shown in Fig. 1.123. Two additional point masses, each of mass m, are attached to the disc at radius R and at axial locations z=+l and z=−l, respectively. The two masses are arranged diametrically opposite so that the rotor remains in static balance (the net centrifugal force is zero). The rotor turns at constant angular velocity ω about the shaft. Use a fixed inertial frame with unit vectors i and j in the plane perpendicular to the shaft and unit vector k along the shaft. Let θ(t)=ω t denote the azimuth of the mass at z=+l, and define the radial unit vector in the transverse plane as e_r(t)=cos θ(t) i+sin θ(t) j. The bearings are located at z=+d and z=−d. Define F_c as the magnitude of the centrifugal force on each added mass, T as the magnitude of the couple (torque) produced by the two centrifugal forces about the mid-plane, F_b as the magnitude of the transverse force applied by each bearing to supply an equal and opposite couple, and F_+(t) and F_−(t) as the vector forces at the bearings located at z=+d and z=−d, respectively. Find the time-varying forces F_+(t) and F_−(t).

subject: physics
problem: Question: A 100 \unit{m^2} solar panel is coupled to a flywheel such that it converts incident sunlight into mechanical energy of rotation with efficiency \eta = \frac{1}{100}. Assume the solar irradiance (solar constant) is 2 \unit{cal/cm^2/min} and remains constant for the full interval. Use the conversion 1 \unit{cal} = 4.2 \unit{J}. The flywheel is a homogeneous solid cylinder of mass m = 500 \unit{kg} and radius R = 50 \unit{cm} = 0.5 \unit{m}, with moment of inertia about its symmetry axis I = \frac{1}{2} m R^2. Symbols used in the subproblems and solutions: \omega is the angular speed, \omega_0 is the angular speed after the 8 \unit{h} exposure, \omega_1 is the angular speed when slipping ceases, v is the center-of-mass speed, v_1 is the center-of-mass speed when slipping ceases, f is the magnitude of the kinetic friction force, \mu is the coefficient of kinetic friction, g is the magnitude of gravitational acceleration, t is the time measured from the release, t_1 is the time when slipping ceases, S is the distance traveled until slipping ceases, and Q is the energy dissipated as heat. Take g = 9.8 \unit{m/s^2}.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A man wishes to break a long, slender, uniform rod by hitting it against a rock. The end O of the rod held in his hand is an instantaneous center that remains fixed in space (rotates without translation), as shown in Fig. 1.125. The man wishes to avoid having a large force act on his hand at the moment the rod hits the rock. Which point on the rod should strike the rock so that the reaction transmitted to the hand is negligible? Ignore gravity. Definitions: l is the length of the rod, m is its mass, c is the center of mass, x is the distance from O to the point of impact along the rod, F(t) is the contact force exerted by the rock on the rod during the short impact, F'(t) is the reaction force at O transmitted to the hand during the impact, J = ∫ F dt is the impulse of F over the impact, J' = ∫ F' dt is the impulse at O over the impact, v is the velocity of c immediately after the impact, ω is the angular velocity about c immediately after the impact, and I is the mass moment of inertia of the uniform rod about c with I = (1/12) m l^2. The required final answer is x, the distance from O to the point of impact

subject: physics
problem: Question: Two flywheels on parallel frictionless shafts initially do not touch. The larger wheel has spin rate f = 2000 \unit{rev/min} and the smaller wheel is at rest. The shafts are moved until the rims come into contact, and the system evolves to equilibrium with no further sliding at the contact (pure rolling). Given R_1 = 2 R_2 and I_1 = 16 I_2, find the angular velocity of the smaller wheel after equilibrium. Definitions: R_1 and R_2 are the radii of the larger and smaller wheels, respectively; I_1 and I_2 are their moments of inertia about their axes; \omega_1 is the initial angular velocity of the larger wheel before contact; \omega_1' and \omega_2' are the angular velocities of the larger and smaller wheels after equilibrium; f is the given initial spin rate with \omega_1 = f; J denotes the contact linear impulse transmitted during the interaction.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Two uniform solid cylinders are spinning independently about their parallel fixed axes. Cylinder 1 has radius R_1 and mass M_1; cylinder 2 has radius R_2 and mass M_2. Initially they rotate in the same sense with angular speeds \Omega_1 and \Omega_2, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1.128. They are then displaced until they touch along a common tangent and are allowed to interact through friction until a steady state with no slip at the contact is reached. Let \omega_1 and \omega_2 denote the final angular velocities of cylinders 1 and 2 after steady state is reached. Let I_1 and I_2 be their moments of inertia about their symmetry axes; for uniform solid cylinders I_i = \frac{1}{2} M_i R_i^2. Let J_1 and J_2 denote the time-integrated friction torques (angular impulses) exerted by the contact on cylinders 1 and 2 during the transient. Find \omega_1 and \omega_2 in terms of M_1, M_2, R_1, R_2, \Omega_1, and \Omega_2

subject: physics
problem: Question: Three identical solid cylinders, each of radius R and moment of inertia I about its own central axis, initially rotate with the same angular velocity Ω about parallel central axes in the same sense. They are brought together until they touch each other in a row, keeping the axes parallel. A new steady state is achieved when, at each contact line, a cylinder does not slip with respect to its neighbor, as shown in Fig. 1.129. The precise order in which cylinder pairs first touch is irrelevant. Let T be the total initial spin kinetic energy and T' be the total final spin kinetic energy after the no-slip steady state is established. Define Ω' as the final angular velocity of cylinder 1; by the no-slip condition at the two contact lines, cylinder 2 has final angular velocity −Ω' and cylinder 3 has final angular velocity Ω'.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Two torsion pendula are identical except that in the second pendulum two solid cylinders are rigidly attached on the face of the original solid disc, as shown in Fig. 1.130. The main disc has radius R and mass M. Each added cylinder is a solid disc of radius R/4 and mass M, and the center of each added cylinder lies at a distance 3R/4 from the central rotation axis of the main disc; the two added cylinders are placed diametrically opposite one another. Each pendulum is suspended by a wire with the same torsional constant A, so that for a small angular displacement θ the restoring torque is Aθ. Let T1 and T2 be the small-oscillation periods of the first and second pendulum, respectively. Define the required period ratio as R_T=T1/T2. Find R_T.

subject: physics
problem:
Question: A long thin uniform bar of mass M and length L is hung from a fixed frictionless axis at point A, as shown in Fig. 1.131. The bar rotates in a vertical plane about A. The moment of inertia of the bar about A is I, given by
$$I=\frac{M L^2}{3}$$
Point B lies on the bar a distance a below A along the bar. An instantaneous horizontal impulse J may be applied at B. During the impulse, the axis at A may exert a horizontal impulse J' on the bar. The bar is initially at rest before any impulse is applied. Let ω0 be the angular velocity just before an impulse and ω be the angular velocity just after. Let v be the speed of the center of mass immediately after the impulse, and let Δp be the change in the bar's linear momentum during the impulse. The center of mass is at the midpoint of the bar, a distance L/2 from A

subject: physics
problem: Question: Two equal point masses M are connected by a massless rigid rod of length 2A (a dumbbell). The center of the rod is fixed at the origin of the laboratory frame. An axle is fixed along the z-axis, and the rod makes a fixed angle θ with the axle. The dumbbell rotates about the z-axis with constant angular speed ω directed along the positive z-axis. At t=0 the dumbbell lies in the xz-plane. Use two right-handed Cartesian frames: the laboratory frame with unit vectors e_1', e_2', e_3' along x, y, z, and a body-attached rotating frame with unit vectors e_1, e_2, e_3. At t=0 the two frames coincide. The rotation between frames is represented by the orthogonal matrix S(t) about e_3'. The position vectors of the two masses are r_1(t) and r_2(t). The linear momenta are p_1(t) and p_2(t). The inertia tensor in the laboratory frame is I(t) with components I_{ij}(t), and its zz-component is I_{zz}(t). The total angular momentum is L(t). The torque on the axle is τ(t). The kinetic energy is T. The angular velocity vector in the laboratory frame is ω_vec = (0, 0, ω). Components are taken with respect to the laboratory basis e_1', e_2', e_3'.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A thin square plate with side length a and mass m rotates at a constant angular frequency ω about a fixed axis through the center. The axis is the z'-axis of an inertial laboratory frame (x', y', z') and is tilted by an angle θ with respect to the normal to the plate. Attach a body-fixed frame (x, y, z) to the plate with origin at the center o, the x- and y-axes along the sides of the square, and the z-axis along the normal to the plate. The unit vectors of these frames are e_x, e_y, e_z and e_{x'}, e_{y'}, e_{z'}, respectively. The axes e_x, e_z, and e_{z'} are coplanar. Let t denote time. Let J denote the angular momentum vector and M denote the torque vector. Use I_{xx}, I_{yy}, and I_{zz} for the principal moments of inertia about the x-, y-, and z-axes, respectively. The angular frequency ω has units \unit{rad/s}, the mass m has units \unit{kg}, and the length a has units \unit{m}. Express vector answers in both the body-fixed frame and the laboratory frame where asked.
answer: M=-\frac{1}{12} m a^2 \omega^2 \sin \theta \cos \theta \left(-\sin \omega t \, e_{x'}+\cos \omega t \, e_{y'}\right)

subject: physics
problem: Question: A thin flat rectangular plate of mass M and sides a by 2a rotates with constant angular velocity ω about a rigid axle through two diagonal corners A and B, as shown in Fig. 1.138. The axle is supported at A and B by bearings that can exert forces only on the axle. Ignore gravitational and frictional forces. Let O be the center of mass. Use a body-fixed right-handed frame Oxyz with y normal to the plate, z along the long side 2a, and x along the short side a. Let e_x, e_y, e_z be the unit vectors of Oxyz. The axis of rotation z′ lies in the plane of the plate through A and B, and α is the angle between z and z′, with sin α = 1/√5 and cos α = 2/√5. Let O x′ y′ z′ be obtained by rotating Oxyz about the y-axis so that z′ coincides with the axle AB, with y′ = y and x′ completing the right-handed triad. Denote by N_A and N_B the forces exerted by the bearings on the axle at A and B, respectively, and by N_{Ax′}, N_{Ay′}, N_{Bx′}, N_{By′} their components in O x′ y′ z′. Let d = (1/2) AB = (√5/2) a be the half-distance between the bearings measured along the axle. Finally, let O x″ y″ z″ be an inertial frame with z″ = z′ and with x″ coinciding with x′ at t = 0. Find the forces exerted by the two bearings as functions of time, expressed in components along x″ and y″.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A thin ring of mass M and radius R is pivoted at point P on a frictionless horizontal table, as shown in Fig. 1.140. A bug of mass m runs along the ring with constant speed v relative to the ring. The bug starts from the pivot P while the ring is initially at rest. When the bug reaches the diametrically opposite point on the ring (point x), find its speed relative to the table. Use the following notation: I is the moment of inertia of the ring about P; ω is the angular velocity of the ring about P at the instant the bug reaches point x; J is the angular momentum of the ring about P; v_table is the speed of the bug relative to the table at point x

subject: physics
problem: Question: A rigid cone of height h and base radius R is constrained to rotate about its vertical symmetry axis. A thin, straight groove is cut along the cone surface from the apex to the base (along a generatrix). The cone is set rotating with initial angular velocity ω_0 about its axis. A small point-like bead of mass m is released at the apex into the frictionless groove and is permitted to slide downward under gravity. Assume the bead always remains in the groove during the motion. The moment of inertia of the cone about its axis is I_0. The gravitational acceleration is g, directed downward. When the bead reaches the base, let ω denote the cone's angular velocity at that instant. Let v_{\parallel} be the bead's velocity component along the groove and v_{\perp} the component perpendicular to the groove (azimuthal, due to the cone's rotation). Let i and j be unit vectors perpendicular and parallel to the groove, respectively. Define the bead's laboratory-frame velocity vector at the base as v_{\text{vec}} = v_{\perp} i + v_{\parallel} j, and define the speed magnitude as v_{\text{mag}} so that v_{\text{mag}}^2 = v_{\parallel}^2 + v_{\perp}^2.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A thin uniform disc of radius a and mass m rotates freely on a frictionless bearing with uniform angular velocity of magnitude ω about a fixed vertical axis z′ passing through its center O. The axis z′ is inclined at angle α to the symmetry axis of the disc. Introduce a body-fixed orthonormal frame Oxyz with origin at O, the z-axis along the disc’s normal, the x-axis in the plane spanned by z and z′, and the y-axis completing a right-handed triad. Let e_x, e_y, e_z be the unit vectors along x, y, z, respectively. Let Ω denote the angular velocity vector, with magnitude ω and components in the body frame specified by Ω = ω sin α e_x + ω cos α e_z. Let I_x, I_y, I_z denote the principal moments of inertia of the disc about the x, y, z axes, respectively. Let L denote the angular momentum of the disc about O. Let M denote the torque exerted by the axis on the disc, defined by M = (dL/dt)_{fixed}, where (d/dt)_{fixed} is the time derivative in the inertial frame and (d/dt)_{rot} is the time derivative in the rotating body frame Oxyz. Let F denote the net force exerted by the axis on the disc. Determine the magnitude and direction of M and F.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A pendulum consists of a uniform rigid rod of length L and mass M, and a bug of mass M/3 that can crawl along the rod. The rod is pivoted at one end and swings in a vertical plane. Let theta(t) be the small angle from the vertical, with initial value theta_0 at t=0, where theta_0 << 1 \unit{rad}. The bug is initially at the pivot, so l(0)=0, and the system is released from rest. For t>0 the bug crawls slowly outward along the rod toward the free end with constant speed V, where V=dot l and l(t) in [0,L]. Let g be the gravitational acceleration. Define omega(l) as the instantaneous small-oscillation angular frequency when the bug is at distance l. Let E(l) be the energy of oscillation and E_0=E(0). Let T and U denote the kinetic and potential energies of the oscillation, respectively. Let theta_max be the oscillation amplitude at the instant l=L. Overbars denote averages over one oscillation period. A dot denotes a time derivative. Let I be the moment of inertia of the rod-plus-bug system about the pivot. Let f be the force exerted by the rod on the bug along the rod direction. Let W be the work done by f on the surroundings for an outward displacement dl. Let K be the integration constant that appears when integrating the energy balance.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A uniform solid bowling ball is projected along a horizontal alley with initial center-of-mass speed v_0 at t=0 so that it initially slides without rolling. The ball has mass m and radius R. The coefficient of static friction with the floor is mu_s and the coefficient of kinetic (sliding) friction is mu_d. Let g be the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration. Ignore air resistance. While the ball is sliding, let f denote the magnitude of the kinetic friction force, a the linear acceleration of the center of mass, alpha the angular acceleration about the center, I the moment of inertia about the center, and t the time at which the ball first transitions to rolling without slipping. Let v_roll denote the linear speed at that instant. Compute v_roll in terms of v_0. Use I = \frac{2}{5} m R^2 for a uniform solid sphere and the rolling-without-slipping condition v = R omega at the transition, with omega = alpha t.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A uniform coin of mass m and radius R is initially spinning about its axis of symmetry with angular frequency ω. At time t=0 it is gently set down on a horizontal surface. The coefficient of sliding friction between the coin and the surface is μ, and g is the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration. Choose coordinates as in Fig. 1.149 with the x-axis in the plane of the surface. Let x_c(t) be the x-coordinate of the center of mass and θ(t) the rotation angle about the symmetry axis, with θ increasing in the sense of the initial spin so that ω>0. The coin’s moment of inertia about its symmetry axis is I=1/2 m R^2. During slipping, kinetic friction of magnitude μ m g acts along the negative x direction. Let t_s be the time when slipping ceases and pure rolling begins. Define v_f as the x-component of the center-of-mass velocity at t=t_s. Find v_f, expressed as v_f=...

subject: physics
problem: Question: A uniform solid wheel (disk) of mass M and radius R moves on a horizontal surface. Its center of mass is projected to the right with initial linear velocity V_0, and it has an initial angular velocity about its axle of magnitude ω_0 such that this initial spin tends to produce rolling opposite to the translational motion. Let x(t) be the horizontal coordinate of the center of mass and θ(t) the rotation angle. Define the positive x direction to the right and define positive angular velocity to be clockwise. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the wheel and the surface is μ, assumed constant, and g denotes the magnitude of the acceleration due to gravity. The wheel’s moment of inertia about its axle is (1/2) M R^2. Pure rolling (no slipping) begins when the linear speed of the center of mass equals R times the angular speed.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A thin hollow cylinder of radius R and mass M slides across a frictionless floor with initial forward speed V_0 along a fixed straight line. The cylinder initially spins backward relative to the forward direction. Let V(t) be the signed linear velocity along the line of motion, where V>0 denotes the initial forward direction, and let ω(t) be the signed angular velocity about the cylinder’s axis, where ω>0 corresponds to the sense that would produce forward rolling. The cylinder then passes onto a rough horizontal segment and continues along the same line. During slipping, a kinetic friction force of magnitude f(t) acts at the contact point opposite the direction of slipping. Let t=0 be the instant the cylinder enters the rough segment, and let t_0 be the instant when pure rolling without slipping begins. The moment of inertia about the symmetry axis is I = M R^2 for a thin hollow cylinder. The initial conditions are V(0) = V_0 and ω(0) = -ω_0 with ω_0 = 2 V_0 / R. Define the final signed linear velocity and angular velocity at the onset of rolling as V_f = V(t_0) and ω_f = ω(t_0). Find V_f. Report the final answer as V_f, where a negative value indicates motion opposite to the initial direction.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Calculate the minimum coefficient of static friction mu_min necessary to keep a thin circular ring (hoop) from sliding as it rolls without slipping down a plane inclined at an angle theta with respect to the horizontal. Let the ring have mass m and radius R. Let x(t) be the coordinate of the ring’s center measured along the incline, and varphi(t) be the ring’s rotation angle about its symmetry axis. Let g be the magnitude of gravitational acceleration with units \unit{m/s^2}. Let f be the magnitude of the static friction force acting along the plane, and N be the normal reaction of the plane. The ring’s moment of inertia about its symmetry axis is I = m R^2. Let mu be the coefficient of static friction between the ring and the plane.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A solid uniform cylinder of mass m and radius R is placed on a plane inclined at angle \theta relative to the horizontal, as shown in Fig. 1.154. Let g denote the magnitude of the acceleration due to gravity. Let a be the linear acceleration of the cylinder’s center of mass along the incline. The coefficient of static friction between the cylinder and the plane is \mu. Define \theta_c as the critical angle below which the cylinder rolls without slipping. Let f denote the static friction force along the plane, N the normal force exerted by the plane on the cylinder, and \alpha the angular acceleration about the cylinder’s central axis. Assume the positive direction is down the incline.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A wheel of radius r, mass m, and moment of inertia I equal to m R squared is pulled along a horizontal surface by application of a horizontal force F to a rope unwinding from an axle of radius b, as shown in Fig. 1.155. You may assume there is a frictional force between the wheel and the surface such that the wheel rolls without slipping. In the expression I = m R squared, the quantity R is a constant with dimensions of length (the radius of gyration). Use x(t) for the horizontal position of the wheel’s center and theta(t) for its rotation angle about the center. Take the positive x-direction to the right and positive theta as counterclockwise. Let f denote the static friction force at the contact point, defined positive when it opposes the forward motion of the wheel. The no-slip rolling constraint relates the translational and rotational motions.

subject: physics
problem:
Question: A flat uniform disc lies on a frictionless horizontal table. The disc has mass m = 9/5\unit{kg} and radius r = 1/5\unit{m}. A string wound around the cylindrical rim of the disc exerts a horizontal force f = 3\unit{N} directed toward the north (see Fig. 1.156). Use a for the magnitude of the acceleration of the center of mass and specify its direction. Use alpha for the angular acceleration about the center of mass and specify its sense. Use I for the moment of inertia of the disc about its center, defined by
$$
I = \frac{m r^2}{2}
$$
Find the magnitude and direction of a and alpha. Determine whether the relation
$$
a = r \alpha
$$
holds, and explain.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A wheel of radius $R$ and moment of inertia $I$ about a fixed, frictionless axle through point $O$ has a light, flexible cord wrapped around its rim. The cord supports a body of mass $M$ that descends vertically without slipping, as shown in Fig. 1.157. Let $F$ be the tension in the cord, $x$ the downward displacement of the body, $\theta$ the rotation angle of the wheel (positive in the direction of descent), and $g$ the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration. Find the tension $F$ in the cord during the motion.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Two uniform solid discs in a vertical plane have masses M_1 and M_2 and radii R_1 and R_2, respectively. A light inextensible thread is wound about their circumferences and connects them as shown in Fig. 1.158. Disc 1 rotates about a fixed frictionless horizontal axle through its center. Assume the thread does not slip on either disc. Define x(t) as the vertical distance of the center of mass of disc 2 from the center of disc 1, measured positive downward. Define theta_1(t) and theta_2(t) as the rotation angles of discs 1 and 2, respectively, increasing in the sense that pulls the thread downward on disc 2. Let F be the tension in the thread, g the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration, and I_1 and I_2 the moments of inertia of discs 1 and 2 about their centers. For uniform discs, I_1 = (1/2) M_1 R_1^2 and I_2 = (1/2) M_2 R_2^2. Set up the equations needed to determine the acceleration d^2 x/dt^2 of the center of mass of disc 2 as it falls freely

subject: physics
problem: Question: A yo-yo is modeled as two identical solid disks of radius R and thickness t, separated by a distance t, connected by a solid cylindrical shaft (spool) of radius r and length t. The yo-yo has uniform volumetric mass density ρ, total mass M, and moment of inertia I about its central axis. A massless string is wound around the shaft and the yo-yo descends vertically under gravity with acceleration g. Let x be the downward displacement of the center of mass, θ the angular displacement as the string unwinds, and F the tension in the string. Assume the string unwinds without slipping so that x = r θ and hence ddot{x} = r ddot{θ}. Find the tension F in the string during the descent.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A homogeneous solid sphere of mass M and radius R, with moment of inertia about its center I = \frac{2}{5} M R^2, rests on the horizontal platform of a truck. The truck starts from rest and moves with constant horizontal acceleration A relative to the ground. The sphere rolls without slipping on the platform. Use two coordinate frames: an inertial ground frame O'x'y' and a truck-attached frame Oxy. The x and x' axes are horizontal and parallel. Let x be the x-coordinate of the sphere's center of mass in the truck frame Oxy, and x' its x-coordinate in the ground frame O'x'y'. Let \xi be the truck platform's x-displacement relative to the ground, so that x' = x + \xi and \ddot \xi = A. Let f be the static friction force acting on the sphere along the x direction at the contact with the platform. Let \theta be the rotation angle of the sphere about its center. Rolling without slipping in the truck frame gives \dot x = - R \dot \theta, with dots denoting time derivatives. Find the acceleration of the sphere's center of mass relative to the truck, i.e., find \ddot x.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Referring to Fig. 1.160, find the minimum initial height h, measured vertically above the topmost point of a vertical loop of radius R, that will permit a solid spherical ball of radius r and mass m, rolling without slipping, to maintain constant contact with the rail throughout the loop. The moment of inertia of the solid sphere about its center is I = 2/5 m r^2. Let g denote the acceleration due to gravity. Let v and ω denote, respectively, the speed of the sphere’s center of mass and its angular velocity about its center when the sphere reaches the top of the loop. Let T denote the total kinetic energy of the sphere at the top of the loop.

subject: physics
problem:
Question: A solid homogeneous sphere of mass m, radius a, and moment of inertia about its center I is initially at rest at the top of a fixed cylinder of radius b. The sphere rolls without slipping on the outer surface of the cylinder until it loses contact. The distance from the cylinder center to the sphere center is a+b. Let g be the gravitational acceleration and t be time. Let θ be the angular position of the sphere's center measured from the top of the cylinder, with θ=0 at the top and θ increasing in the direction of motion. Let \varphi be the rotation angle of the sphere about its center. Let N be the normal force exerted by the cylinder on the sphere, and f be the tangential friction force. Assume static friction is sufficient to prevent slipping before the sphere loses contact. The rolling-without-slipping constraint holds until separation. Define θ_{max} as the angle at which the sphere leaves the cylinder. Let v denote the speed of the sphere's center. Define unit vectors e_r (radially outward from the cylinder center to the sphere center) and e_t (tangent to the cylinder in the direction of increasing θ). Let v_r and v_t be the components of the center-of-mass velocity along e_r and e_t, respectively. The sphere's moment of inertia is
$$
I=\frac{2}{5} m a^2
$$

subject: physics
problem: Question: In Fig. 1.163, two identical uniform solid spheres (each of mass M and radius R) move on a horizontal plane. Ball 1 initially rolls without slipping toward ball 2, which is initially at rest. Let V be the initial center-of-mass speed of ball 1. The collision is instantaneous and perfectly elastic. During the collision interval, frictional forces are negligible so the impulsive interaction is along the line through the centers. Let I be the moment of inertia of each sphere about its center, with I = (2/5) M R^2. Let V_1 and V_2 denote the translational speeds of balls 1 and 2, respectively, and let omega_1 and omega_2 denote their angular velocities, all taken just before the collision. A single prime (') denotes quantities immediately after the collision. A double prime ('') denotes quantities long after the collision when each ball is again rolling without slipping. The positive directions for linear and angular variables are as shown in Fig. 1.164.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A small homogeneous sphere of mass m and radius r rolls without sliding on the outer surface of a larger stationary sphere of radius R, as shown in Fig. 1.165. The origin is at the center of the large sphere with the z-axis vertical. The polar angle of the small sphere is θ, measured from the top of the large sphere. The small sphere starts from rest at θ = 0. Let g be the gravitational acceleration. Let t denote time. Let v(θ) denote the speed of the center of the small sphere as a function of θ. Let N be the normal reaction on the small sphere and f the tangential friction force. Let μ be the coefficient of friction. Let θ_c be the angle at which the small sphere loses contact with the large sphere, defined by N = 0. Let θ_s be the angle at which the small sphere first starts to slide. Let φ be the rotation angle of the small sphere about its center. Let ω be the angular speed of the small sphere about its center, related to v during rolling without slipping by ω = v/r. Let I be the moment of inertia of the small sphere about its center; for a solid homogeneous sphere I = (2/5) m r^2. The distance from the large-sphere center to the small-sphere center is R + r.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A uniform slender plank (rod) of length 2a and mass m is held so that one end leans against a frictionless vertical wall and the other end rests on a frictionless horizontal floor, making an initial angle \theta_0 with the floor. When the plank is released from rest at \theta = \theta_0, it slides under gravity while remaining in contact with the wall and the floor. Let \theta(t) denote the instantaneous angle of the plank with the floor, and let g denote the acceleration due to gravity. The center of mass is at the midpoint, a from either end. Overdots denote time derivatives with respect to time t. For part (b), let x(t) denote the horizontal coordinate of the center of mass measured from the lower end along the floor, and let N_1 be the normal reaction force exerted by the wall on the upper end.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A thin uniform stick of length L and mass m has its lower end in contact with a perfectly smooth horizontal table. It is released from rest at an angle θ_0 to the vertical at time t=0. Let N denote the vertical normal reaction force exerted by the table on the stick at the contact point, g the acceleration due to gravity, θ(t) the angle the stick makes with the vertical, and y(t) the vertical coordinate of the center of mass measured upward from the table. The stick’s moment of inertia about a horizontal axis through its center of mass is I_cm = (1/12) m L^2. Find N at an infinitesimally small time after release (t=0^+).

subject: physics
problem: Question: Two long uniform rods A and B each of length L=1\unit{m} lie parallel to each other on a frictionless horizontal plane with axes (x,y). Rod B is initially at rest along y=0 from x=0 to x=1\unit{m}. Rod A, of mass m_A=1\unit{kg}, moves in the positive y direction with speed 10\unit{m/s} and initially extends from x=-1+\epsilon to x=\epsilon\unit{m}, where 0<\epsilon\ll 1\unit{m}. At time t=0 the right end of A reaches y=0 and A collides elastically with the left end of B. Rod B has mass m_B=2\unit{kg}. Ignore any subsequent collisions after the first contact. Define the following unknowns for the motion immediately after the collision: v_A and v_B are the y-components of the center-of-mass velocities of rods A and B, respectively, and \omega_A and \omega_B are their angular velocities about their centers of mass. Let I denote the scalar impulse delivered by A to B during the collision, directed along +y. Use the standard moment of inertia of a uniform rod of length L about its center of mass, I_{cm}=(1/12)mL^2. Define the initial total kinetic energy as E_i and the final total kinetic energy as E_f. Find v_A, \omega_A, v_B, and \omega_B after the collision, and verify energy conservation by checking E_f=E_i.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A solid billiard ball of radius R and mass M is initially at rest on a horizontal table. It is struck by a horizontal cue stick at a height h above the table surface, as shown in Fig. 1.171. The moment of inertia of the ball about its center is I = 2/5 M R^2. Find the value of h such that immediately after the impulsive hit the ball will roll without slipping. Definitions for the analysis: f is the magnitude of the impact force exerted by the cue, Δt is the contact duration, Δv is the change in the center-of-mass speed, Δω is the change in the angular speed about the center, v is the center-of-mass speed immediately after impact, and ω is the angular speed immediately after impact. The rolling-without-slipping condition is v = R ω, and the torque lever arm about the center during impact is h − R.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A uniform solid ball of mass m and radius a rolls without slipping on a level surface with initial center-of-mass speed v and angular speed ω, so v = a ω. It collides inelastically with a vertical step of height h satisfying 0 < h < a at the impact point A, as shown in Fig. 1.172. During the impulsive contact, there is no slipping at A and the center of mass is momentarily at rest immediately after the impact, so the ball instantaneously rotates about A with angular speed ω'. Let J and J' denote the angular momentum about A just before and just after the collision, respectively. Let I_cm = (\frac{2}{5}) m a^2 be the moment of inertia of the solid sphere about its center, and let I' = I_cm + m a^2 be the moment of inertia about a horizontal axis through A. Let g denote the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration. Find, in terms of a, h, and g, the minimum initial speed v_min for which the ball will trip up over the step.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A parked truck has its rear door wide open as shown in the plane view in Fig. 1.173. Model the door as a uniform slender rod of length L and mass m, hinged at one end to the truck body. The truck begins motion at time t=0 with constant acceleration of magnitude a in the plane of the door. Let the door’s orientation be described by the angle theta(t), measured from its initial orientation, with initial conditions theta(0)=0 and dtheta/dt(0)=0. Overdots denote time derivatives with respect to t. The hinge exerts a force on the door with components F_parallel (parallel to the door in the plane) and F_perp (perpendicular to the door in the plane). Define the center-of-mass acceleration components along those directions as a_perp_cm and a_parallel_cm, respectively. The door is considered closed when theta reaches pi, and the total elapsed time from t=0 to closing is denoted by t. In part a of the first subquestion, Delta_t denotes a later time at which the door passes through the position shown in Fig. 1.173. For consistency, any occurrence of F_bot in the subproblems refers to the same perpendicular component denoted here by F_perp.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A stepladder consists of two identical uniform legs, each modeled as a slender rod of length l and mass m, held together by a hinge at the top at point A and a horizontal rope near the bottom. The legs rest on a horizontal, frictionless surface at 60^\circ as shown in Fig. 1.175. At the instant the rope is suddenly cut, find the acceleration of the hinge A. Use an inertial coordinate system with x horizontal and y vertically downward. For one leg, let B be the contact point with the floor at the bottom, and C its center of mass. Let \theta(t) be the angle of the leg with the horizontal, with \theta=60^\circ at that instant. Let I be the mass moment of inertia of one leg about C. Let N be the vertical normal reaction of the floor on the leg at B, and F the horizontal interaction force exerted by the other leg on this leg at the hinge A. Let a_A denote the magnitude of the vertical acceleration of point A, and let a_{Cx}, a_{Cy} be the components of the acceleration of C. Take g as the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration with units \unit{m/s^2}.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A particle of mass m and speed v collides elastically with the end of a uniform thin rod of mass M and length l, as shown in Fig. 1.177. The rod is initially at rest. After the collision, the particle of mass m is stationary. Calculate M. Variables used in the solution: v_c is the velocity of the center of mass of the rod after the collision, ω is the angular velocity of the rod about its center of mass after the collision, and I is the moment of inertia of the rod about its center of mass.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A uniform thin cylindrical rigid rod of length L and mass m is supported at its ends by two massless vertical springs with spring constants k_1 and k_2. In equilibrium the rod is horizontal, as shown in Fig. 1.178. Consider small-amplitude motion about equilibrium under circumstances where the springs and the rod can move only vertically. Use y_1 and y_2 for the vertical displacements of the left and right ends of the rod, measured upward from the equilibrium configuration. Seek normal-mode solutions of the form y_1 = A_1 e^{i \omega t} and y_2 = A_2 e^{i \omega t}, where A_1 and A_2 are complex amplitudes, t is time, i is the imaginary unit, and \omega is the angular eigenfrequency. The rod is allowed to translate vertically and to rotate about its center. Let I denote the moment of inertia of the rod about its center for rotation about an axis perpendicular to the rod. For a uniform rod

subject: physics
problem: Question: A rigid conical trash-can lid is supported by a pivot at the center O. Let C be the center of mass, and let r denote the vector from O to C, with magnitude r_0=|r|. The lid is tipped and spun rapidly with angular velocity ω about its symmetry axis. Let m be the mass of the lid, g the gravitational acceleration vector of magnitude g pointing vertically downward, L the angular momentum of the lid about O (in an inertial frame), M the torque of gravity about O, and ω_p the precession angular velocity vector about the vertical (parallel to g). For a rapidly spinning symmetric cone, L is directed along the symmetry axis. Define the sense variables s_ω and s_p as follows: when viewed from above along the vertical axis, s_ω=+1 for counterclockwise spin and s_ω=-1 for clockwise spin; s_p is defined analogously for the precession about the vertical. Determine whether the lid precesses in the same or opposite sense to the spin by giving the relation between s_p and s_ω. Document your answer with the governing equations and refer to the vector diagram in Fig. 1.182.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A rigid, horizontal, massless square frame carries four identical disks rotating as shown in Fig. 1.183. Each disk has mass m, moment of inertia I_0 about its own spin axis, and spin angular speed \omega_0. The frame pivots freely about a support at one corner O. Let c be the center of mass of the four-disk system (located at the square's center), and let D=|OC| be the distance from O to c. The spin directions are as in Fig. 1.184 so that the resultant spin angular momentum of the system is directed along OC and is horizontal. Let g denote the gravitational acceleration vector (of magnitude g, directed downward). Let L denote the total angular momentum of the system about O, M the torque about O due to gravity, \Omega the vector precessional angular velocity, and \Omega_p its magnitude (the precession rate). Find \Omega_p and the sense of precession as seen from above.

subject: physics
problem: Question: We consider an ideal free symmetric gyro, i.e., a rotationally symmetric rigid body with principal moments of inertia I_1 = I_2 < I_3, suspended so it can rotate freely about its center of mass with no external torque. Let ω(t) denote the instantaneous angular velocity vector, L(t) denote the instantaneous angular momentum vector, and u(t) denote the unit vector along the body symmetry axis associated with I_3. Work in an inertial fixed frame Oxyz with unit vectors i, j, k, and in a rotating body frame Ox^{\prime}y^{\prime}z^{\prime} with unit vectors i^{\prime}, j^{\prime}, k^{\prime}, where k^{\prime} is aligned with the symmetry axis. The orientation is described by the Euler angles θ, \varphi, ψ as in Fig. 1.185. Choose the fixed frame at t = 0 with the z-axis along L(0) and the y-axis perpendicular to ω_0 = ω(0). Choose the body frame at t = 0 with k^{\prime} along the symmetry axis u_0 = u(0) and i^{\prime} in the plane spanned by k^{\prime} and k, so that initially y^{\prime} aligns with y and \varphi(0) = 0, ψ(0) = 0. Define θ_0 = θ(0). Define u_0 = u(0) with components u_{0x}, u_{0y}, u_{0z} in the fixed frame. Define ω_0 = ω(0) with components ω_{0x}, ω_{0y}, ω_{0z} in the fixed frame and ω_{0 x^{\prime}}, ω_{0 y^{\prime}}, ω_{0 z^{\prime}} in the body frame. Let L_m = |L(t)| and ω_m = |ω(t)| denote magnitudes. Let α be the angle between ω(t) and the fixed z-axis, and β the angle between ω(t) and the body z^{\prime}-axis. Derive explicit expressions for L(t), ω(t), and u(t) in terms of the initial values u_0 and ω_0
answer: ω(t) = ω_{0x} \cos \left( \frac{d\varphi}{dt} t \right) i + ω_{0x} \sin \left( \frac{d\varphi}{dt} t \right) j + ω_{0z} k

subject: physics
problem: Question: A thin disk of mass M and radius A is connected by two identical springs of spring constant k to two fixed points on a frictionless tabletop. Each spring has unstretched length l_0, and in the symmetric equilibrium configuration each is stretched to length l > l_0. Consider small oscillations confined to the plane of the table. Let x denote the small displacement of the disk’s center of mass along the common line of the springs from its equilibrium position, and let θ denote the small angular displacement of the disk about its center. Let F_1 and F_2 denote the tensions in the left and right springs, respectively. Let I be the moment of inertia of the disk about its center, and let φ be the small geometric angle defined in the figure between a spring and the radius from the disk’s center to the point of spring attachment. Let ω_1 and ω_2 be the angular frequencies of the two normal modes, and let f_1 and f_2 be the corresponding frequencies in hertz defined by f = ω/(2π). Find ω_1 and ω_2 for small oscillations, and describe the motion in each normal mode.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A heavy symmetric top with one point fixed is precessing at a steady angular velocity Ω about the vertical axis z. The symmetry axis of the top is z′, which is inclined at an angle θ with respect to the z-axis. The spin about the symmetry axis z′ is denoted by ω′. The top has mass m, its center of gravity is at a distance h from the fixed point, and the acceleration due to gravity is g. Use the coordinate systems indicated in Fig. 1.193, with the axes z, z′, x, and x′ in the same plane at the time under consideration. Assume the principal moments of inertia satisfy I1 = I2 and let I3 be the moment of inertia about the symmetry axis z′. Let the Euler angles be φ (precession about z), θ (inclination between z and z′), and ψ (spin about z′). Time derivatives are denoted by d/dt. In steady precession, θ is constant, the precession rate about z is Ω, and the spin rate about z′ is ω′. Let ω′min denote the minimum spin about z′ required for steady precession. Determine ω′min in terms of m, g, h, I1, I3, and θ.

subject: physics
problem: Question: The game of Jacks is played with metal pieces that can be approximated by six equal point masses m located at distances l from the center along the positive and negative directions of three orthogonal body-fixed principal axes x', y', z', as shown in Fig. 1.194. The total mass is M=6m. Gravity acts downward with magnitude g and units \unit{m/s^2}. The length parameter l has units \unit{m}. The orientation of the jack is described by Euler angles (varphi, theta, psi): varphi is the precession angle about the vertical, theta is the angle between the vertical and the body z' axis, and psi is the spin angle about the body z' axis. Define the spin angular velocity s=dot psi and the precession rate Omega=dot varphi. The body angular velocity vector omega has components (omega_{x'}, omega_{y'}, omega_{z'}) in the body frame. The gravitational torque vector tau has components (tau_{x'}, tau_{y'}, tau_{z'}) in the body frame. In steady precession about the vertical (Fig. 1.195), the analysis below relates s, Omega, and theta. For convenience when indicating constancy, we will also use the statement omega_{z'} is constant during steady precession.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A propeller-driven airplane flies in a horizontal circle, counterclockwise when viewed from above, with a constant angular velocity χ with respect to an inertial frame. Its propeller turns at a constant angular velocity dψ/dt clockwise as seen by the pilot. Use an inertial frame Oxyz with the z-axis vertical up, and a rotating frame o x'y'z' fixed to the propeller with the x'-axis along the propeller spin axis and the z'-axis along a propeller blade. At the instant under consideration, take the x-axis to coincide with the x'-axis. The principal moments of inertia about x', y', z' are I1, I2, I3, respectively; for a flat, four-bladed propeller by symmetry I2 = I3, and define I = I2 = I3. Let ω denote the angular velocity of the propeller expressed in the rotating frame with components ω_{x'}, ω_{y'}, ω_{z'}. Let M denote the torque exerted on the propeller shaft with components M_{x'}, M_{y'}, M_{z'}. The propeller spin angle is ψ with ψ̇ = dψ/dt and ψ = ψ̇ t, and the airplane’s turn rate is χ.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A perfectly uniform rigid ball of diameter 20 \unit{cm} and density 5 \unit{g/cm^3} rotates freely in space at 1 \unit{rev/s}. An intelligent flea of mass 10^{-3} \unit{g} lives in a small massless house fixed to the ball's surface at a rotational pole, as shown in Fig. 1.197. The flea quickly walks to latitude 45^{\circ} and waits there. Neglect the small precession during the flea's motion. How long should it wait so that the ball's equator passes through the house? Use the following definitions: R is the ball's radius, M is the ball's mass, m is the flea's mass, \rho is the ball's density, \omega is the angular speed magnitude before the flea moves, t is time, t_{wait} is the required waiting time, I_1=I_2 and I_3 are the principal moments of inertia about body axes Ox'y'z' with z' through the flea's new position, \omega_{x'}, \omega_{y'}, \omega_{z'} are the components of the angular velocity in the body frame, and \Omega is the body's torque-free precession rate about z'. Take an inertial frame Oxyz with z along the original \omega and a body-fixed frame Ox'y'z' attached to the ball with x and x' in the plane of z and z' at t=0, as in Fig. 1.198. The center of mass is at the ball's center O since m \ll M, and \omega at t=0 equals its value just before the flea moved.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A horizontal uniform bar of mass m and length 2a hangs by two parallel, inextensible strings of length 2a attached to its two ends. The strings are fixed at support points located at (a, 0, 2a) and (-a, 0, 2a) in a right-handed Cartesian frame (x, y, z). At t = 0 the bar lies along the x-axis in the plane z = 0 and is suddenly given an angular velocity ω about the vertical z-axis through its center. Let g be the acceleration due to gravity. Let h denote the maximum vertical rise of the bar's center of mass relative to its initial level. Let T(t) denote the tension in each string and let ΔT denote the initial increase in T over its static value mg/2. Let I be the moment of inertia of the bar about the vertical axis through its center. Let E denote the total mechanical energy. Let θ(t) be the angle the bar makes with the x-axis in the xy-plane, and z(t) be the height of the bar's center of mass. Define the end of the bar at x > 0 as point A and its corresponding support as A′, with coordinates A = (a cos θ, a sin θ, z) and A′ = (a, 0, 2a). Derivatives with respect to time t are written using d/dt. Calculate

subject: physics
problem: Question: A string is stretched between two rigid supports that are 100 cm apart. Let the string length be L and the wavelengths corresponding to the only excitable frequencies in the 100 Hz to 350 Hz range, namely 160 Hz, 240 Hz, and 320 Hz, be λ_0, λ_1, and λ_2, respectively. The string has fixed ends, so it supports standing waves with mode index n, a positive integer. Determine λ_0, λ_1, and λ_2.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A taut string of length l, linear mass density rho (mass per unit length), and uniform tension F is considered. Let y(x,t) denote the transverse displacement of the string, and let theta(x,t) be the small angle the string makes with the x-axis. Let v be the wave speed along the string, lambda the wavelength of a standing wave, and omega the angular frequency of a vibrational mode. Consider a short segment of horizontal length Delta x and arc length Delta l; the tensions at its two ends have magnitudes F_1 and F_2, directed tangentially to the string and making angles theta and theta+Delta theta with the x-axis, respectively. Let f_x and f_y denote the x- and y-components of the net force on this short segment. In the small-oscillation limit, |theta| is small and Delta l approximates Delta x. For standing-wave modes with fixed ends, the boundaries are at x=0 and x=l.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A violin string of length L is fastened at both ends. The fundamental (open string) frequency is f_0. The violinist bows the string at a distance L/4 from one end and touches the string lightly at the midpoint L/2. Let lambda_0 denote the wavelength corresponding to f_0 for the open string. Under the imposed bowing and touching conditions, let lambda denote the allowed wavelength of an excited mode. Let f_min denote the lowest frequency that can be excited under these conditions, and let f_1 denote the frequency of the first overtone under these conditions. All answers should be expressed in terms of f_0.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A guitar string is fixed at both ends and vibrates in its fundamental mode. The string length is l = 80 cm, the fundamental frequency is f = 400 Hz, the maximum transverse displacement at the middle is y0 = 2 cm, and the tension is T = 10^6 dyne. Use Cartesian coordinates with the x-axis along the equilibrium position of the string and the origin at the left support x = 0. Let y(x,t) be the transverse displacement at position x and time t. Let θ(x,t) be the small angle that the string makes with the x-axis; at the support we write θ(t) = θ(0,t). The component of the force on the left support that is perpendicular to the equilibrium position is denoted F_y(t). Assume small slopes so that sin θ ≈ θ ≈ ∂y/∂x. Let v be the wave speed, λ the wavelength, and ω the angular frequency, with λ = 2l in the fundamental mode. Find the maximum value F_{y,max} of F_y(t).

subject: physics
problem: Question: A transverse traveling sinusoidal wave on a long stretched wire with mass per unit length \(\rho\) (SI units \unit{kg/m}) has angular frequency \(\omega\) (SI units \unit{rad/s}) and wave speed \(c\) (SI units \unit{m/s}). The maximum transverse displacement amplitude is \(y_0\) with \(y_0 \ll \lambda\), where \(\lambda\) is the wavelength, and the wave propagates in the +\(x\) direction. Let \(y(x,t)\) denote the transverse displacement at position \(x\) (SI units \unit{m}) and time \(t\) (SI units \unit{s}). Let \(E\) denote the energy per unit length (energy density) along the wire, \(P\) the power transmitted along the wire, and \(F_y(t)\) the transverse force applied at \(x=0\) by the generator. Let \(\Delta_t\) denote a time interval.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A piano string of length l is fixed at both ends. The string has linear mass density sigma and is under tension T. Let y(x,t) denote the transverse displacement of a point located at position x along the string at time t, where 0 \leq x \leq l. The wave speed is v = \sqrt{T/\sigma}. In the separation-of-variables method we write y(x,t) = X(x) A(t) with separation constant k. Mode indices n and m denote positive integers. The nth-mode period, frequency, and wavelength are denoted by T_n, \nu_n, and \lambda_n, respectively. In part (b) the string is initially displaced by a small distance s at x = l/2 so that s \ll l, forming an isosceles triangle, and then released from rest. The coefficients A_n and B_n are Fourier sine series coefficients for the normal modes.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A spring of rest length X and force constant k has total mass m. One end is fixed at x=0 and the other end at x=X is attached to a point mass M. The spring lies horizontally, and M moves on a frictionless surface. Let x denote the coordinate along the spring from the fixed end. Let \xi(x,t) be the longitudinal displacement field of the spring measured from equilibrium. Let a be the cross-sectional area of the spring and \sigma its Young's modulus. Define K_0 = a \sigma and the mass density per unit length \rho = m/X. The fixed-end boundary condition is \xi(0,t) = 0. The end x = X is attached to M, so the boundary condition there follows from Newton's second law for M. The wave speed along the spring is denoted by v, the angular frequency by \omega, the phase by \varphi, and the wavenumber by K. When solving the spatial equation, A and B denote constants of integration.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Consider small transverse oscillations y(x,t) of a light, flexible string of length l and uniform mass per unit length ρ. Let x measure distance along the string from the end attached to a vertical pole at x=0 to the other end at x=l. Let T(x) denote the static tension distribution along the string. Let τ denote a constant end tension when the string is held at both ends under uniform tension. Let F_y(x) denote the internal transverse force in the string at position x. Let Δx denote a small element of string length used for force balance. In the rotating case, let ω be the angular frequency of steady rotation about the pole, and let Ω be the angular eigenfrequency of a small transverse oscillation about the steady configuration. Define the dimensionless coordinate ξ = x/l. Let P_n(ξ) denote the Legendre polynomial of integer degree n. Unless otherwise stated, gravity is neglected, oscillations are small, and y is measured perpendicular to the x-axis.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A long, uniform string of linear mass density μ is held under constant tension T. Small-amplitude transverse waves of angular frequency ω propagate along the string from left to right. At x=0, a point mass m is attached to the string. Let R denote the fraction of the incident energy flux that is reflected by the inhomogeneity. In part (b), the point mass m is replaced by a short string segment of length l and linear mass density μ_m with μ_m >> μ; its mass equals m, so an equivalent length λ is defined by λ = m / μ_m. The wave speed on the uniform string is v = sqrt(T/μ).

subject: physics
problem: Question: A perfectly flexible string with uniform linear mass density ρ and length L hangs from a fixed support at x=0 with its bottom end free at x=L, as in Fig. 1.214. Use a right-handed coordinate system Oxyz with the x-axis directed downward along the string. Let y(x,t) denote the small transverse displacement of the string in one plane, t is time, T(x) is the tension in the string at position x, and g is the gravitational acceleration. For separation of variables, write y(x,t)=ξ(x)τ(t), where ξ(x) is the spatial mode shape and τ(t) is the time-dependent amplitude. Let λ>0 be the separation constant determined by the boundary conditions. The boundary conditions for the transverse motion are y(0,t)=0 (top fixed) and y(L,t) is finite (bottom end free). In part (b), a power-series solution ξ(x)=∑ a_n (x−L)^n about x=L is used, with coefficients a_n determined by a recurrence. Define f(z)=∑_{n=0}^{∞} (-1)^n z^n/(n!)^2, and use Newton's method α_{k+1}=α_k−f(α_k)/f'(α_k) to approximate the smallest positive root of f(λL)=0, where α_k denotes successive approximations to λL. Let ν be the oscillation frequency in hertz, and ν_min the frequency of the lowest normal mode. Constants A and B denote arbitrary constants determined by initial conditions. Let Δx denote a small element length of the string used in the derivation.

subject: physics
problem: Question: An infinite string with constant tension T and linear mass density \sigma undergoes small transverse displacements y(x,t). Define the wave speed by v=\sqrt{T/\sigma}. The initial displacement and velocity are given by y(x,0)=f(x) and \frac{\partial y}{\partial t}(x,0)=g(x), respectively. Find the motion y(x,t) for all t>0. In the solution below, f_1 and f_2 are auxiliary functions, C is an integration constant, and f_1'(x)=\frac{d f_1}{dx}(x), f_2'(x)=\frac{d f_2}{dx}(x).

subject: physics
problem: Question: A long wave packet with amplitude A, composed predominantly of angular frequencies very near the central value ω_0, propagates on an infinitely long string of linear mass density μ and tension T, as shown in Fig. 1.218. A bead of mass m is attached to the string at x = 0, as shown in Fig. 1.219. Let y(x,t) denote the small transverse displacement of the string. The wave speed is denoted by v, and for a monochromatic component of angular frequency ω, the wavenumber is k = ω/v. For waves near ω_0 we write, for x < 0, y_1(x,t) as a superposition of an incident wave of amplitude A and a reflected wave of amplitude B; for x > 0, y_2(x,t) is a transmitted wave of amplitude C. The angles that the string makes with the x-axis immediately to the left and right of the bead are θ_1 and θ_2, respectively. The imaginary unit is i with i^2 = -1, and C^* denotes the complex conjugate of C. The magnitude of the complex transmitted amplitude is |C|, defined by |C| = sqrt(C^* C). The task is to find the transmitted amplitude |C| and, in the limit of large m and large ω_0, determine how |C| scales with ω_0.

subject: physics
problem: Question: An organ pipe of length l, open at both ends and aligned with the airflow, is used in a subsonic wind tunnel to measure the Mach number M of air in the tunnel, where M=v/c and v is the uniform airflow speed while c is the speed of sound in still air. When the pipe is fixed in the tunnel, it resonates with a fundamental period t (the same quantity denoted by δt in the original statement). Let t0 be the fundamental period of the pipe in still air. Assume the fundamental mode of an open-open pipe has wavelength λ=2l and that the effective wave speed relative to the pipe is the speed of sound in still air adjusted by the pipe’s motion relative to the medium. Given v/c=1/2, calculate the ratio of periods t/t0.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Two media have a planar, perfectly impermeable interface at z=0 as shown in Fig. 1.223. Plane acoustic waves of pressure amplitude A and frequency f are generated in medium (1), propagating normally toward medium (2). Medium (1) has density \(\rho_1\) and sound speed \(c_1\), while medium (2) has density \(\rho_2\) and sound speed \(c_2\). Let the z-axis be perpendicular to the interface with the origin at the interface. Let p(z,t) denote the acoustic pressure and v(z,t) the particle velocity along z. Define the acoustic impedance in medium j as \(Z_j=\rho_j c_j\). The incident, reflected, and transmitted pressure amplitudes are A, A_r, and B, respectively. The angular frequency is \(\omega=2\pi f\).

subject: physics
problem: Question: Let the speed of sound in air be c. A point source of sound moves at constant speed v along the +x direction through still air. The inertial frame Oxy is fixed to the air with origin O at x = 0, y = 0. Time is t. At time t the source is at the point S on the +x axis. Define a source-fixed frame Sx'y' with origin at S and axes parallel to x and y. The angle phi parameterizes points on circles centered at O by x = r cos phi and y = r sin phi. The semi-vertex angle of the cone in part (b) is denoted by theta.

subject: physics
problem: Question: An airplane flies horizontally at constant speed v = 600 \unit{m/s} at a constant altitude h = 8000 \unit{m}. The speed of sound in air is c = 300 \unit{m/s}. An observer stands at point A on the ground. Let A' be the point on the airplane's straight-line path directly above A, so AA' = h. Define x to be the horizontal distance A'S from A' to the airplane's position S at the instant the observer hears the sonic boom. Using the geometry of the Mach cone for v > c, find x. Points used in the solution: O is the airplane's position when the sound that produces the sonic boom heard at A was emitted; S is the airplane's position when the observer at A hears the boom; A is the observer; A' lies on the airplane's path directly above A. Let t be the time interval between emission at O and hearing at A. Refer to Fig. 1.226 and Fig. 1.227.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Consider a plane standing sound wave in air at temperature T=300 K and frequency nu=10^3 Hz. The amplitude of the excess pressure (pressure variation relative to ambient) is p_0=1 dyn/cm^2, and the ambient pressure is p_a=10^6 dyn/cm^2. Let xi(x,t) be the longitudinal displacement field of the air along the x direction, with amplitude xi_0. Define k as the wave number, omega as the angular frequency, v as the speed of sound in air, p(x,t) as the instantaneous excess pressure, B as the bulk modulus of air, and rho as the mass density of air. To relate strain to displacement, consider a small cylindrical element of air with cross-sectional area A and initial length Delta x, having initial volume V=A Delta x and volume change Delta V caused by the displacement field. Let m be the mass of that element, mu the molar mass of air, and R the universal gas constant. Use SI units in calculations. Use the following data for air: mu=29×10^{-3} kg/mol, R=8.31 J/mol/K, v=340 m/s.

subject: physics
problem: Question: An acoustical motion detector emits a continuous sound at frequency nu=50\unit{kHz}. The speed of sound in air is c=330\unit{m/s}. The detector registers a moving object if the received echo frequency differs from the emitted frequency by at least the threshold Delta nu_th=100\unit{Hz}. Assume one-dimensional motion along the line of sight and a simple reflection from the object. Let v denote the object's line-of-sight velocity relative to the detector, taken as positive when the object moves toward the detector. Let nu' be the sound frequency in the object's rest frame, and let nu'' be the echo frequency received by the detector after reflection. Find the minimum speed magnitude v_min such that the detector registers motion, and give the registration condition in the form |v|>=v_min.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A student near a railroad track hears a train's whistle as the train approaches directly and then as it recedes directly. Let nu_0 be the frequency emitted by the whistle, nu_1 the frequency heard when the train is approaching, and nu_2 the frequency heard when the train is receding. The observed frequencies are nu_1 = 250 \unit{Hz} and nu_2 = 200 \unit{Hz}. Let c = 360 \unit{m/s} be the speed of sound in air, and let v be the speed of the train relative to the air. Find v in SI units.

subject: physics
problem: Question: The velocity of blood flow in an artery can be measured using Doppler-shifted ultrasound. Assume the ultrasound beam is incident at a very small angle to the blood flow, so the flow can be treated as directly along the beam away from the transducer (see Fig. 1.229). Let nu be the incident ultrasound frequency, c be the speed of sound in tissue, v be the blood speed along the beam (away from the transducer), nu'' be the frequency of the wave after reflection back to the transducer, and Delta nu=nu''-nu be the required frequency shift. Given nu=1.5\times 10^6\unit{Hz}, v=1\unit{m/s}, and c=1500\unit{m/s}, calculate Delta nu.

subject: physics
problem:
Question: A car has forward- and backward-directed speakers mounted on its roof and drives toward a stationary observer with speed 50\unit{ft/s}, as shown in Fig. 1.230. Both speakers are driven by an oscillator of frequency 1000\unit{Hz}. A brick building is located behind the car and reflects the backward-emitted sound toward the observer. Take the speed of sound to be 1000\unit{ft/s}. Define c as the speed of sound, v as the car speed, 
u as the oscillator frequency, 
u_f as the frequency of the direct sound from the front-directed speaker received by the observer, 
u_b as the frequency of the echo from the backward-directed speaker received by the observer, and 
u_{beat} as the beat frequency heard by the observer. Find 
u_{beat} between the direct sound and the echo.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A light, inextensible rope is attached at one end to a fixed wall and is wrapped around a capstan through a total contact angle \theta, measured in radians. A person pulls on the free end of the rope with a steady force F, as shown in Fig. 1.231. The coefficient of static friction between the rope and the capstan is \mu_s. Define T_w as the rope tension in the straight segment between the wall and the capstan (the high-tension side). For analysis along the wrap, define the contact-angle coordinate \phi measured from the free end toward the wall, with 0 \le \phi \le \theta, and let T(\phi) denote the rope tension as a function of \phi. Take F and T_w in \unit{N}, and \theta and \phi in \unit{rad}.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A seasoned oak cantilever beam of rectangular cross section 2\unit{in} × 4\unit{in} is built into a concrete wall and extends a distance l = 6\unit{ft} to a free end P. The beam supports a downward end load L at P. For least bending, the 4\unit{in} dimension is placed vertically, so the height is 2h = 4\unit{in} and the width is w = 2\unit{in}. Assume pure bending and neglect shear stresses. The elastic (yield) limit in normal stress is T_max = 7900\unit{lb/in^2}. The Young's modulus is E = 1.62 × 10^6\unit{lb/in^2}. Let x be the distance from the wall (x = 0 at the wall, x = l at P), y(x) the vertical deflection of the neutral axis, r the radius of curvature of the neutral plane, ξ the signed distance from the neutral axis within the cross section, I the second moment of area about the neutral axis, M(x) the internal bending moment, and T(ξ) the longitudinal normal stress at distance ξ. Use the small-deflection curvature approximation 1/r ≈ - d^2 y/dx^2. Find the largest load L_max that can be supported without exceeding T_max anywhere in the beam, and the corresponding deflection of point P, y_P = y(l). Use l = 72\unit{in} in calculations.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A bucket of water is rotated at a constant angular velocity ω about its symmetry axis (the z-axis). After steady rotation is reached, determine the equation z(r) for the free surface of the water. Use cylindrical coordinates (r, φ, z) with the origin at the lowest point of the surface and z measured upward. Let m be the mass of a small water element at the surface, g the magnitude of the gravitational acceleration, F the magnitude of the contact force exerted by neighboring water on that element and directed along the surface normal, and θ the angle between the surface normal and the z-axis. For reference, ω has units \unit{rad/s}, r and z have units \unit{m}, and g has units \unit{m/s^2}.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A device consists of a thin vertical tube connected to a wide horizontal tube as shown in Fig. 1.240. The device is immersed in a surrounding liquid of density \rho_f. The external atmosphere has density \rho_a and pressure p_a. The open end of the horizontal tube is sealed, and the device is then rotated with constant angular velocity \omega about a vertical axis passing through the junction. Treat the air in the horizontal tube as an ideal gas at fixed temperature T, with molar mass M and universal gas constant R. Ignore capillarity, surface friction, and any variation of density with altitude. Let g be the acceleration due to gravity. Let the horizontal tube have uniform cross-sectional area S and extend from the rotation axis at x=0 to the sealed end at x=L, where x is the radial coordinate along the tube. Let p(x) and \rho(x) denote the air pressure and density in the horizontal tube at position x, and let p_0 and \rho_0 denote their values at x=0. Let h be the equilibrium rise height of the surrounding liquid in the thin vertical tube relative to its nonrotating level. Find h to second order in \omega (i.e., retain terms up to order \omega^2 and neglect terms of order \omega^4 and higher).

subject: physics
problem: Question: Given that the apparent angular diameters of the Moon and the Sun as seen from Earth are approximately equal and that the tides raised by the Moon are about twice as high as those raised by the Sun, determine the ratio of their average densities. Use the following definitions and notation: R_e, R_m, R_s are the radii of Earth, Moon, and Sun; M_e, M_m, M_s are their masses; D_m, D_s are the distances from Earth's center to the Moon and the Sun; h_m, h_s are the heights of the tides raised by the Moon and the Sun at the same point on Earth; theta is the Moon's (or Sun's) zenith distance at that point; G is the gravitational constant; g is the acceleration due to gravity at Earth's surface; rho_m, rho_s are the average densities of the Moon and the Sun. Assume the tide-raising potential is dominated by the quadrupole term. The approximate equality of angular diameters implies R_m/D_m = R_s/D_s. The observed tidal ratio implies h_m/h_s = 2.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A hypothetical material out of which an astronomical object is formed has an equation of state p = (1/2) K rho^2, where p is the pressure, rho is the mass density, and K is a positive material constant. The object is in hydrostatic equilibrium under its self-gravity. The gravitational potential is denoted by phi, and G is the gravitational constant. We will also use F for the external force per unit volume, f for the external force per unit mass, and d tau for a fluid volume element. In part c we assume spherical symmetry about r = 0, with r the radial coordinate, R the equilibrium radius, and e_r the radial unit vector. We introduce the substitution u(r) = rho r and the parameter omega defined by omega^2 = 4 pi G / K. The symbols u_0 and beta are integration constants, C is a constant of integration, and n is a positive integer.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Consider a self-gravitating slab of fluid in hydrostatic equilibrium with total thickness 2h and infinite lateral extent in the x and y directions. The mass density is ρ(z), a function of z only, and the distribution is symmetric about the midplane z=0, i.e., ρ(z)=ρ(-z). Let p(z) denote the pressure and G the gravitational constant. Assume the surface is in vacuum so that p(h)=0. Define the half-column density σ by σ=∫_0^h ρ(z) dz. Let p_mid denote the pressure at the midplane z=0, i.e., p_mid=p(0).
