problem: Question: A particle of mass m is confined to one-dimensional motion along the x-axis between two perfectly reflecting walls. The walls move symmetrically toward the center at constant speed V, with V much smaller than the particle's speed. The particle's speed is always much less than the speed of light. Let x(t) denote the instantaneous separation between the walls and let x0 = x(0). Let p denote the magnitude of the particle's linear momentum and p0 = p(0) when the separation is x0. Collisions with the walls are perfectly elastic.

subject: physics
problem: Question: The Poisson bracket for functions a and b of canonical variables q_k and p_k is defined by $$[a,b]=\sum_k\frac{\partial a}{\partial q_k}\frac{\partial b}{\partial p_k}-\sum_k\frac{\partial a}{\partial p_k}\frac{\partial b}{\partial q_k}$$ Consider a two-dimensional oscillator with Cartesian coordinates x and y, mass m, and constants K and C. The kinetic and potential energies are $$T(\dot x,\dot y)=\frac{1}{2}m(\dot x^2+\dot y^2)$$ $$V(x,y)=\frac{1}{2}K(x^2+y^2)+Cxy$$ The Lagrangian is $$L=T-V$$ Let H denote the Hamiltonian constructed from L. An overdot denotes a time derivative. Canonical momenta are conjugate to their corresponding coordinates, for example p_x and p_y are conjugate to x and y, and p_\eta and p_\xi are conjugate to \eta and \xi introduced later. In part (e), indices i and j take values in {1,2}, with x_1=x, x_2=y, p_1=p_x, and p_2=p_y.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A particle of mass m moves in one dimension with coordinate q(t) in a potential energy field V(q) and is retarded by a linear damping force F_d = -2 m gamma dot q proportional to its velocity. Let t denote time and gamma > 0 be the damping coefficient. Use p for the momentum conjugate to q, H for the Hamiltonian, F_2(q,P,t) for a type-2 generating function, and (Q,P) for the transformed canonical variables with transformed Hamiltonian K(Q,P,t). For the harmonic oscillator, use V(q) = (1/2) m omega^2 q^2. In the underdamped case gamma < omega, define omega_1 = sqrt(omega^2 - gamma^2). Let A and varphi denote integration constants determined by initial conditions.

subject: physics
problem: Question: We consider a single particle moving in one spatial dimension with canonical coordinate q and canonical momentum p under a Hamiltonian H. We use a type-2 generating function S(q, \alpha, t) to perform a canonical transformation to new variables (\beta, \alpha), where \beta is the transformed coordinate and \alpha is the transformed momentum. The transformed Hamiltonian is denoted by K and is related to H by K = H + \partial S/\partial t evaluated on the transformation. Time is denoted by t, and all time derivatives are written using d/dt. The symbols \alpha_0 and \phi are integration constants determined by initial conditions. When we write q[\beta(t), \alpha(t)] and p[\beta(t), \alpha(t)], we mean the original variables expressed in terms of (\beta, \alpha) and then evaluated on the time-dependent solutions (\beta(t), \alpha(t)). If a symbol \Delta_t appears in any subproblem statement, it should be understood as the time variable t.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A rectangular solid block of length l along the x-direction and cross-sectional area A on the face parallel to the applied shear force F (see Fig. 2.77) is subjected to a tangential shearing force F. The small transverse displacement field is u(x,t), directed along the y-direction. The shear angle is denoted by phi, the shear modulus by n, and the mass density by rho. Within elastic limits, linear elasticity (Hooke's law for shear) applies. Energies and variational quantities: T is the kinetic energy, V is the potential energy, L is the Lagrangian L = T - V, and V_v denotes the potential energy per unit volume (energy density). The time bounds for Hamilton's principle are t_1 and t_2, and delta u is the variation of u with delta u = 0 at x = 0, x = l, t = t_1, and t = t_2. The wave speed is denoted by v. Units: F in \unit{N}, A in \unit{m^2}, l in \unit{m}, u in \unit{m}, phi dimensionless, n in \unit{Pa}, rho in \unit{kg/m^3}, x in \unit{m}, t in \unit{s}, v in \unit{m/s}.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A spacecraft moves at constant speed v relative to Earth along the +x direction. Let Σ (Earth frame) and Σ′ (spacecraft frame) be inertial frames. A standard clock at the Earth origin records time t, and a standard clock at the spacecraft origin records time t′. The clocks are synchronized at the event t = 0, t′ = 0, x = 0, x′ = 0. The Earth observer watches his own clock directly and the spacecraft clock through a telescope. Define Δ_t to be the reading of the Earth clock at the instant he visually sees the spacecraft clock’s display. Given β = v/c and γ = 1/√(1 − β^2), and c the speed of light, when the spacecraft clock reads 1 hour, find Δ_t.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A light source at rest at position x = 0 in inertial frame S emits two light pulses, P_1 at time t = 0 and P_2 at time t = \tau. Another inertial frame S' moves with constant velocity v along the +x direction relative to S. Define c as the speed of light in vacuum, \beta = v/c, and \gamma = 1/\sqrt{1-\beta^2}. An observer in S' located at x' = 0 receives the initial pulse P_1 at time t' = 0. Let \Delta t' denote the light-propagation time from the emission event of P_2 to its reception at x' = 0. In parts (b) and the second (a), \nu and \nu' are the pulse frequencies in S and S', and \lambda and \lambda' are the corresponding vacuum wavelengths. In part (c), consider the hydrogen H_\beta emission line with rest wavelength \lambda = 4861.33 \unit{\AA} produced by neutralization to H atoms of protons accelerated through a potential of 20 \unit{kV}. Assume emission occurs after acceleration while the protons drift with constant velocity, and that the spectrometer optical axis is parallel to the proton motion. Let E be the proton kinetic energy after acceleration, m the proton rest mass, and mc^2 its rest energy. In part (c), let u denote the proton drift speed so that the relevant Doppler parameter is \beta = u/c. The wavelength shift is denoted by \Delta \lambda; the first- and second-order contributions are denoted by \Delta \lambda_1 and \Delta \lambda_2

subject: physics
problem: Question: A spaceship is moving away from Earth at speed v = (4/5)c. When the ship is at a distance of 6.66 × 10^8 km from Earth as measured in the Earth's inertial frame Σ, an observer on Earth sends a radio signal toward the spaceship. Let c = 3 × 10^5 km/s, let β = v/c, and let γ = 1/√(1 − β^2). Let x_s denote the Earth-frame separation between Earth and the spaceship at the instant the signal is emitted (so x_s = 6.66 × 10^8 km in Σ). Determine how long it takes for the signal to reach the ship and where the ship is when the signal is received, as measured in both Σ (Earth frame) and Σ′ (ship frame).

subject: physics
problem: Question: An atomic clock is carried once around the world by a jet plane and then compared with a previously synchronized and similar clock that did not travel. Approximately how large a discrepancy does special relativity predict? Use the following definitions and assumptions: Let S be the Earth's approximately inertial frame with coordinates (x, t). Let S' be the aircraft's rest frame with coordinates (x', t'). The aircraft has constant speed v relative to S with v=1000\unit{m/s}. Let c be the speed of light with c=3\times 10^8\unit{m/s}. Define β=v/c and γ=1/\sqrt{1-β^2}. For the two events corresponding to departure and arrival of the aircraft's clock, define Δt as the time interval between these events in S and Δt' as the proper time interval measured by the aircraft's clock in S'. Define the predicted special-relativistic discrepancy as Δt_dis=Δt−Δt'. Let the Earth's radius be R=6400\unit{km}. The circumnavigation path length is 2πR and the circumnavigation time in S is T=2πR/v. Neglect gravitational effects and accelerations and use special relativity only. Report the final answer as Δt_dis in seconds.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Consider two inertial frames \Sigma and \Sigma' with parallel axes. The frame \Sigma' moves with constant velocity v=\beta c along the +x direction relative to \Sigma, and the origins coincide at t=t'=0. Use c for the speed of light and \gamma=1/\sqrt{1-\beta^2}. The position 4-vector is x^\alpha=(x,y,z,ct). The momentum 4-vector is p^\alpha=(p_x c,p_y c,p_z c,E), where m denotes the relativistic mass so that E=m c^2 and (p_x,p_y,p_z) is the 3-momentum. The wave 4-vector is k^\alpha=(k_x c,k_y c,k_z c,\omega), with angular frequency \omega and frequency \nu related by \omega=2\pi\nu. In part (b), take \Sigma' as the rest frame of the source and \Sigma as the observer's frame. Let \beta_0 be the relative speed of the source with respect to the observer divided by c. Approaching corresponds to \beta=-\beta_0, receding corresponds to \beta=\beta_0, and the transverse case corresponds to the source-observer line of sight being perpendicular at the instant of closest approach.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A monochromatic transverse wave is emitted by a source at rest in the inertial frame κ. The wave has proper frequency ν and propagates at an angle θ=60^{\circ} with respect to the x-axis in κ. The speed of wave propagation is c. An observer is at rest in frame K', whose x'-axis is parallel to the x-axis of κ. The frame K' moves with speed v=\frac{4}{5}c along the +x direction relative to κ. Define β=\frac{v}{c} and γ=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-β^{2}}}. The observer measures the wave's frequency ν' and its angle of propagation θ' in K'. (a) Determine the measured frequency ν' in terms of the proper frequency ν. (b) Determine the angle of observation θ' in K'.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Two twins exchange radio pulses tied to their heartbeats. Each twin's heart beats once per second and each twin broadcasts one radio pulse at each heartbeat. The stay-at-home twin A remains at rest in inertial frame Σ with spatial x-axis along the line of motion. The traveler twin B starts at rest at time t = 0, then very rapidly accelerates to constant speed v along +x (within less than a heartbeat and without perturbing his heartbeat), coasts, and then instantaneously reverses velocity to return home, arriving at his own proper time 2 t_1. Assume the acceleration phases are negligibly short so that B is inertial between the turnaround events. Pulses are modeled as plane electromagnetic waves propagating along the x-axis. Let c be the speed of light. Let Σ′ be B’s instantaneous rest frame during each inertial leg, and let A and B be located at the spatial origins of Σ and Σ′, respectively, during those legs. Define the following: - Proper half-trip time of B: t_1 (in seconds). - Heartbeat frequency in each twin’s own rest frame: ν_0 = 1 Hz, so each twin emits one pulse per second by his own clock. - Angular frequency and wavevector: ω = 2 π ν and k, with |k| = ω/c. During the outbound leg the pulses from A to B propagate along +x in Σ, while during the inbound leg the pulses from A to B propagate along −x in Σ. Similarly, pulses from B to A propagate along the line connecting them. Tasks for B’s point of view: - Compute the total number of pulses B sends, N_{B,send}. - Compute the number of pulses B receives on the outgoing leg, N_{B,rec,out}. - Compute the number of pulses B receives on the ingoing leg, N_{B,rec,in}. - Compute the ratio R_B = N_{B,rec,total} / N_{B,send}. Tasks for A’s point of view: - Let t_2 be the time in Σ at which A’s received pulses from B switch from Doppler-lowered to Doppler-raised frequency, and let t_3 be the remaining duration in Σ from t = t_2 to the end of the trip. Compute the number of pulses A receives during [0, t_2], denoted N_{A,rec,1}. - Compute the number of pulses A receives during the interval of length t_3, denoted N_{A,rec,2}. - Compute the ratio N_{A,rec,2} / N_{A,rec,1}. - Compute the ratio R_A = N_{A,send,total} / N_{A,rec,total}, where A sends at ν_0 and receives all pulses sent by B. Compare R_A to R_B. Use β = |v|/c and γ = 1 / sqrt(1 − β^2). All times are measured in seconds and frequencies in hertz, with ν_0 = 1 \unit{Hz}.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A spaceship with a transmitter and a receiver moves at constant velocity v directly away from Earth along the +x axis. Let Σ be the Earth rest frame with coordinates (t,x) and Σ' be the spaceship rest frame with coordinates (t',x'). The speed of light is c. Define β=v/c and γ=1/\sqrt{1-β^2}. The spaceship emits a radar pulse at t'=0 and x'=0 that reflects from Earth and is received back on the ship 40 seconds later according to the ship's clock. The received angular frequency equals one half the transmitted angular frequency. Let ω_0 be the transmitted angular frequency measured in Σ'. Let ω be the angular frequency measured in Σ for the outbound wave and let ω'' be the angular frequency measured back in Σ' after reflection. Let k_x' and k_x denote the x components of the wavevector in Σ' and Σ, respectively. Define x'_{bounce} as the Earth position at the reflection event as measured in Σ'. Define t'_{one} as the one-way light travel time in Σ', t'_{bounce} as the Σ' time of the reflection event, and t'_{recv} as the Σ' time of the reception event. Define x_{ship,recv} as the ship position in Σ at the instant the reflected pulse is received.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A point source s of monochromatic light emits radiation of frequency f. An observer A moves at constant speed v along a straight line that passes at a perpendicular distance d from the source. Let o be the point of closest approach, and let x be the signed distance of the observer from o measured along the trajectory, with x>0 in the direction of motion. Let c be the speed of light in vacuum. Define β=v/c and γ=1/√(1−β^2). Let Σ be the rest frame of the source and Σ′ the instantaneous rest frame of the observer. In Σ, the light has angular frequency ω=2πf and wave vector k with magnitude |k|=ω/c. In Σ′, the observed angular frequency and frequency are ω′ and f′ with ω′=2πf′. Let θ be defined by sin θ = x/√(d^2+x^2), which is the projection of the propagation direction of the light onto the x-axis (the direction of motion of A). Use SI units: v and c in \unit{m/s}, d and x in \unit{m}, and f and f′ in \unit{Hz}.

subject: physics
problem:
Question: Consider monochromatic radiation emitted at the Sun with frequency \nu_s in \unit{Hz} and received at the Earth with frequency \nu_e in \unit{Hz}. Work in a static, weak gravitational field described by the metric components

$$
g_{00}=1+\frac{2\Phi}{c^2}
$$

$$
g_{11}=-1
$$

$$
g_{22}=-1
$$

$$
g_{33}=-1
$$

$$
g_{\mu\neq\nu}=0
$$

Here c is the speed of light in \unit{m/s}, and \Phi(r) is the gravitational potential energy per unit mass at position r. Let r_s denote a point on the Sun where the light is emitted and r_e denote a point on the Earth where the light is received. Let P_1 be the emission point and P_2 be the reception point, both at rest in the static field. Let t_0 be the coordinate time of emission of the first crest at P_1, t be the coordinate period between successive emitted crests, and T be the coordinate propagation time from P_1 to P_2. Let \tau be the proper period at P_1 measured by a standard clock co-moving with P_1, and let \tau' be the proper period at P_2 measured by a standard clock co-moving with P_2. Let \nu be the emitted frequency measured locally at P_1 and \nu' be the received frequency measured locally at P_2. Assume the field is static so that T is constant. Using this metric, derive the gravitational redshift, expressed as the fractional frequency shift (\nu_e-\nu_s)/\nu_s, as a function of the difference of gravitational potentials at the Sun and Earth.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A perfectly reflecting planar mirror moves through vacuum with constant relativistic speed v along the +x direction in the inertial frame Σ of the light source and observer. A monochromatic light beam of angular frequency ω_i propagating along the −x direction (i.e., normally incident from x = +∞) strikes the mirror, as shown in Fig. 3.9. Let c be the speed of light. Define β = v/c and γ = 1/√(1 − β^2). Let Σ′ be the rest frame of the mirror. In any frame, ω denotes the angular frequency and k_x the x component of the wavevector; primed symbols ω′ and k_x′ denote the same quantities measured in Σ′. Let ω_r be the angular frequency of the reflected light measured in Σ. Let ω_i′ and ω_r′ be the angular frequencies of the incident and reflected light measured in Σ′. Upon specular reflection in Σ′, the angular frequency is unchanged and the propagation direction reverses, so ω_r′ = ω_i′ and k_x′ changes sign. Let ħ be the reduced Planck constant. Let n be the photon number density measured in Σ. Let P_i be the average incident energy flux measured in Σ, expressed in \unit{W/m^2}, and let P_r be the average reflected energy flux measured in Σ, also in \unit{W/m^2}.

subject: physics
problem: Question: An inertial observer o sees monochromatic photons of frequency ν incident on a perfectly reflecting plane mirror. The mirror lies in the y z plane so that its surface normal points along the +x direction. The incident beam makes an angle θ_i with the surface normal, and the reflected beam makes an angle θ_r with the surface normal. The mirror moves with speed v along the +x direction relative to o. Find θ_r and ν' (the reflected frequency) in terms of θ_i and ν. Then find the result if the mirror instead moves with speed v along the +y direction. Use the following definitions and conventions. The observer frame is Σ and the mirror rest frame is Σ'. The wave four-vector in Σ is (ω/c, k_x, k_y, k_z) and in Σ' is (ω'/c, k_x', k_y', k_z'). The speed of light is c. The dimensionless speed and Lorentz factor are defined by

problem: Question: In an inertial frame S with Cartesian axes x, y, z, two spaceships travel in opposite directions along straight, parallel trajectories separated by a distance d along the x-direction, as shown in Fig. 3.14. The direction of motion of both ships is parallel to the y-axis. Each ship has speed c/2, where c is the speed of light. At the instant of closest approach (the dotted line in the figure), ship (1) ejects a small package. Define S' as the inertial frame comoving with ship (1), with axes x', y', z' parallel to those of S. Let u denote the speed of the package as measured in S, and let u_x, u_y be its velocity components in S; let u_x', u_y' be its velocity components in S'. Let v_1 be the velocity of S' relative to S along the y-direction, and let gamma be the corresponding Lorentz factor. The angle alpha to be reported in part (a) is defined in S' as the angle of the package's velocity measured from the positive x'-axis toward the positive y'-axis. In part (b), let u' denote the speed of the package as measured in S'.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Two particles of equal mass m move along the +x direction in the laboratory frame K_0. Their relativistic momenta in K_0 are p_1 = 5 m c for the slower particle and p_2 = 10 m c for the faster particle. Here c is the speed of light with unit \unit{m/s}. For particle i in K_0, let v_i be its speed, \beta_i = v_i/c, \gamma_i = 1/\sqrt{1 - \beta_i^2}, and p_i its relativistic momentum with p_i = m \gamma_i v_i. Let K_1 be the rest frame of the slower particle and K_2 be the rest frame of the faster particle. Let K' be an inertial frame moving at speed v along +x relative to K_0. For any particle with x component of velocity u_x in K_0, its x component in K' is u_x' = (u_x - v)/(1 - u_x v/c^2). Define v_2' as the speed of the faster particle measured in K_1 and v_1' as the speed of the slower particle measured in K_2.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Two inertial observers (observer 1 and observer 2) have rest frames K and K' with parallel axes. Frame K' moves at constant speed u relative to K along the +z direction. A particle moves in K with speed v along a straight-line trajectory making an angle φ with the z axis of K. The x axis is chosen so that the vectors u and the particle velocity lie in the xz plane. Let c denote the speed of light. Let the particle's velocity components in K be v_x, v_y, v_z, and in K' be v_x', v_y', v_z'. Let the particle's speed in K' be v', and let φ' be the angle between the particle's trajectory and the z' axis so that tan φ' = v_x'/v_z'. Define the Lorentz factor for the relative motion along z by γ = 1/√(1 − (u/c)^2). Derive expressions for v_x', v_y', v_z', v', and tan φ'. Then verify that in the limit v = c the transformed speed satisfies v' = c.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Conventions and definitions used throughout: c is the speed of light in vacuum. The electron rest mass is m_e and, when present, the nuclear rest mass is M. For any particle with three-momentum vector denoted by \vec p and energy E, the invariant relation E^2 = m^2 c^4 + p^2 c^2 holds, where m is the rest mass and p is the magnitude of \vec p. For photons, E = p c. Angles are measured between three-momentum directions in the laboratory frame unless otherwise specified. In part (a): the incident photon has energy E_i and three-momentum \vec p_i with magnitude p_i, and the scattered photon has energy E_f and three-momentum \vec p_f with magnitude p_f. The scattering angle between the incident and scattered photon directions is denoted by \theta. The electron is initially at rest and, after scattering, has energy E_e and three-momentum \vec p_e with magnitude p_e. In part (b): a superscript CM denotes quantities measured in the center-of-mass frame of the created e^- e^+ pair. The symbols E_\gamma^{CM} and \vec p_\gamma^{CM} denote the photon energy and three-momentum in that frame. The photon energy in the laboratory frame is denoted E, and the minimum photon energy required for pair creation is denoted E_{min}. The recoil speed of the nucleus after the process is v, with \beta = v/c and \gamma = 1/\sqrt{1 - \beta^2}.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A cosmic-ray proton collides with a stationary proton, creating an excited system that moves along the +x direction of the laboratory frame Σ with Lorentz factor γ=1000 and speed β c relative to Σ. Let Σ′ be the instantaneous rest frame (center-of-mass frame) of the excited system, moving at speed β c relative to Σ along +x. In Σ′ a meson is emitted with speed parameter \bar \beta, i.e., speed \bar \beta c, at polar angle \bar \theta measured from the +x′ axis. In Σ the meson is observed at angle θ from the +x axis. Notation: u_x and u_y are the meson’s velocity components in Σ and u_x′ and u_y′ are the components in Σ′. The speed of light is c. The frame speed parameters are β=v/c and γ=1/\sqrt{1-β^2}. The meson’s speed parameter in Σ′ is \bar \beta with \bar \gamma=1/\sqrt{1-\bar \beta^2}. The meson’s rest mass is m with rest energy m c^2=140\unit{MeV}. For the applications below, the meson momentum in Σ′ is \bar p=\frac{1}{2}\unit{GeV/c}. Define θ_{max} as the maximum laboratory angle observed over emission directions in Σ′. Angles may be given in \unit{rad}, \unit{deg}, or \unit{arcmin} as appropriate.

subject: physics
problem:
Question: Consider a high-energy proton beam of momentum p_p striking a target and producing pions. Use c for the speed of light, the dimensionless speed parameter beta = v/c, and the Lorentz factor gamma = 1/sqrt(1 - beta^2). The rest masses are m_pi for the pion and m_p for the proton. The proper mean lifetime of the pion is tau_0. The laboratory frame is denoted by Sigma with coordinates (x, t) and the pion rest frame by Sigma' with coordinates (x', t'). The decay pipe has length l in Sigma and length l' in Sigma'. The detector distance from the decay point is L in Sigma. For particle kinematics, use E for total relativistic energy and p for momentum. In part (b), n(t) is the number of pions remaining at laboratory time t and n_0 = n(0). In part (c), x_1', x_2' are the Sigma' coordinates of the pipe endpoints recorded at the same t' to define l' = x_2' - x_1'; x_1, x_2 are the corresponding Sigma coordinates. In part (d), the charged pion decays pi -> mu + nu with a massless neutrino; M is the pion rest mass, m is the muon rest mass, and q is the common magnitude of the decay momenta in Sigma'. In part (e), a neutrino emitted at angle theta' in Sigma' is observed at angle theta in Sigma; beta' is the neutrino speed factor in Sigma' and equals 1. The detector radius is R, chosen to collect all neutrinos emitted into the forward hemisphere in Sigma' (theta' <= pi/2). The fraction of pions that decay within the pipe is denoted by f.

$$c = 3 \times 10^{8}\unit{m/s}$$

$$p_p = 400\unit{GeV/c}$$

$$m_\pi = 0.14\unit{GeV/c^2}$$

$$m_p = 0.94\unit{GeV/c^2}$$

$$\beta = \frac{v}{c}$$

$$\gamma = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^{2}}}$$

$$\tau_0 = 2.6 \times 10^{-8}\unit{s}$$

$$l = 400\unit{m}$$

$$L = 1.2\unit{km}$$

problem: Question: In a simplified model of a relativistic nucleus-nucleus collision, a projectile nucleus of rest mass m_1 and speed fraction beta_1 (relative to the speed of light) collides head-on with a target nucleus of rest mass m_2 at rest in the laboratory frame S. The composite system recoils at speed beta_0 in S and has center-of-mass (invariant) energy epsilon_0. Assume no new particles are created. Use natural units with c=1 so that energies and masses have the same units. Define gamma_1 = 1/sqrt(1 - beta_1^2). Let E and p denote the total energy and the magnitude of the total three-momentum of the two-nucleus system in any frame. Primes denote quantities in the center-of-mass frame S'. For numerical estimates in part (a), approximate nuclear rest masses by m_1 = A_1 m_u and m_2 = A_2 m_u, where A_1 and A_2 are the mass numbers and m_u is the atomic mass unit with m_u c^2 ≈ 931.5 \unit{MeV}. In part (c), take the +x axis as the initial projectile (forward) direction. In the recoiling composite rest frame S', the emitted proton has speed beta_c and polar angle theta_c measured from +x. In the laboratory frame S, denote its velocity components by beta_{lx} and beta_{ly}, its speed by beta_l, and its emission angle (from +x) by theta_l.

subject: physics
problem:
Question: In high-energy proton-proton collisions, one or both protons may diffractively dissociate into a system consisting of a proton and several charged pions. Consider the reactions

$$p + p \to p + (p + n \pi)$$

$$p + p \to (p + n \pi) + (p + m \pi)$$

Here n and m are the numbers of produced charged pions. In the laboratory frame \Sigma, an incident proton (projectile) of total energy E_0 strikes a proton at rest (target). Work in natural units with c=1. Let E and p denote, respectively, the total energy and the magnitude of the total 3-momentum of the two-particle system in \Sigma, and let E' and p' denote the corresponding quantities in the center-of-mass frame \Sigma'. Let m_p and m_\pi be the proton and charged-pion rest masses. Unless otherwise stated, take

$$m_\pi = 0.140 \unit{GeV}$$

$$m_p = 0.938 \unit{GeV}$$

For each case below, find the minimum projectile energy E_0 required for the reaction to occur.

problem: Question: Consider the elastic scattering of two spinless particles with masses m and \mu as shown in Fig. 3.18. Let the incoming four-momenta be K and P and the outgoing four-momenta be K' and P'. Use the Minkowski metric with signature (+, -, -, -). For any four-vector A = (A_0, \vec A) define A^2 = A_0^2 - \vec A \cdot \vec A. The on-shell conditions are K^2 = K'^2 = \mu^2 and P^2 = P'^2 = m^2. Define the Lorentz invariants s and t by s = (K_0 + P_0)^2 - (\vec K + \vec P)^2 and t = (K_0' - K_0)^2 - (\vec K' - \vec K)^2. Let \theta denote the scattering angle of \vec K in the center-of-mass frame, defined as the angle between \vec K and \vec K'. Obtain the physical (allowed) region in the (s, t) manifold. Compute the boundary curve t(s) and make a qualitative drawing (see Fig. 3.19).

problem: Question: A beam of 10^6 K_l^0 mesons per second, with speed parameter defined by beta ≡ v/c = 1/\sqrt{2}, is observed to undergo coherent regeneration in a lead brick according to the reaction $$K_l^0 + \text{Brick} \to K_s^0 + \text{Brick}$$ The internal state of the brick is identical before and after the reaction, and the directions of motion of the incoming K_l^0 and outgoing K_s^0 are identical. Use the following data: $$m(K_l)=5\times 10^8 \unit{eV/c^2}$$ $$m(K_l)-m(K_s)=3.5\times 10^{-6} \unit{eV/c^2}$$ Define m_l = m(K_l), m_s = m(K_s), v as the meson speed, c as the speed of light, beta = v/c, and gamma = 1/\sqrt{1-\beta^2}. Let E_l and P_l denote the energy and momentum of the incoming K_l^0, and E_s and P_s denote the energy and momentum of the outgoing K_s^0, all in the lab frame. The beam rate is R = 10^6 \unit{1/s}. Let F_beam be the average force exerted by the brick on the beam, and F_brick be the average force exerted by the beam on the brick. Find the magnitude and direction of F_brick, in newtons or in dynes.

problem:
Question: A pi meson (pion) with rest mass m_π has laboratory-frame momentum p_π = 5 m_π c. It makes an elastic collision with a proton of rest mass m_p = 7 m_π that is initially at rest (Fig. 3.20). Let c denote the speed of light. Define the following symbols used below: P is the total momentum of the two-particle system in the laboratory frame, E is the total energy of the two-particle system in the laboratory frame, v_cm is the velocity of the center-of-mass (c.m.) frame relative to the laboratory frame, β_cm = v_cm/c is the corresponding dimensionless speed, E' is the total energy of the system in the c.m. frame, p_π' is the magnitude of the pion momentum in the c.m. frame, and p_p' is the magnitude of the proton momentum in the c.m. frame. In the c.m. frame the magnitudes satisfy p_π' = p_p'. Use the relativistic single-particle energy relation
$$E_i = \sqrt{p_i^2 c^2 + m_i^2 c^4}$$

problem: Question: High-energy neutrino beams at Fermi Laboratory are made by first forming a monoenergetic pi+ (or K+) beam and then allowing the pions to decay via pi+ -> mu+ + nu_mu. The pion mass is 140 \unit{MeV/c^2} and the muon mass is 106 \unit{MeV/c^2}. Adopt natural units with c = 1 so that energies, masses, and momenta are all measured in \unit{MeV}. Consider a laboratory pi+ beam energy E_pi = 200 \unit{GeV}. Let m_pi denote the pion mass and m_mu the muon mass. Let E_pi and p_pi be the pion energy and momentum in the lab. Let E_mu and p_mu be the muon energy and momentum in the lab. Let E_nu and p_nu be the neutrino energy and momentum in the lab. Primes (') denote the pion rest frame, so E_mu', p_mu', E_nu', p_nu' are the corresponding quantities in that frame. Let theta be the lab polar angle of the neutrino relative to the beam axis, and alpha the lab polar angle of the muon. Define E_{nu,max} as the maximum neutrino energy in the lab over emission angle theta. See Fig. 3.21.

problem: Question: Relativistic perfectly inelastic head-on collisions in the lab frame. After the collision, the two particles stick together and move as a single composite. Constants and definitions used throughout: c is the speed of light. For any speed v, define the dimensionless speed beta = v/c. Define the Lorentz factor gamma = 1/sqrt(1 - beta^2). For particle 1 and particle 2, define rest masses m_1 and m_2, lab speeds v_1 and v_2 with beta_1 = v_1/c and beta_2 = v_2/c, and gamma_1 = 1/sqrt(1 - beta_1^2) and gamma_2 = 1/sqrt(1 - beta_2^2). After collision, the composite has rest mass m and lab speed v with beta = v/c and gamma = 1/sqrt(1 - beta^2). Unless otherwise specified, take m_1 = 1\unit{g} and m_2 = 10\unit{g}. Numerical velocity answers may be reported as v = beta c.

problem: Question: A particle of mass m moves with total energy E_0 at constant speed V, which may approach the speed of light c. It collides elastically with a stationary particle of the same mass m. After the collision the two particles have equal kinetic energies and their outgoing momenta make a relative angle \theta. By symmetry, each scattered momentum makes an angle \theta/2 with the incident direction. Let p_0 and E_0 denote the incident particle's momentum and total energy before the collision, respectively. Let E and p denote the total energy and the magnitude of the momentum of each scattered particle after the collision. Let c denote the speed of light.

problem: Question: Use the Minkowski metric with signature (+, -, -, -). For a four-vector A^\mu = (A^0, \vec{A}) and B^\mu = (B^0, \vec{B}), define the Minkowski dot product and the Minkowski square by $$A^\mu B_\mu = A^0 B^0 - \vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}$$ $$X^2 = X^\mu X_\mu$$ Consider the reaction shown in Fig. 3.22 in which particles of masses m_1 and m_2 are incident and particles of masses m_3 and m_4 emerge. Let the corresponding four-momenta be q_1^\mu and p_1^\mu for the incident particles of masses m_1 and m_2, and q_2^\mu and p_2^\mu for the outgoing particles of masses m_3 and m_4. Define the Mandelstam variables by $$s = (q_1 + p_1)^2$$ $$t = (q_1 - q_2)^2$$ $$u = (q_1 - p_2)^2$$ Throughout, on-shell four-momenta satisfy X^2 = m^2 for a particle of mass m. Three-vectors are denoted with arrows, for example \vec{k}, and their magnitudes by k = |\vec{k}|. In the center-of-mass frame, the total three-momentum vanishes. The notation cos \theta refers to the angle between the initial and final three-momenta of the indicated particle.

problem: Question: The following question concerns Newtonian gravity. Use Newtonian gravity and energy conservation throughout, and adopt cgs units. Constants: G = 6.67 × 10^{-8} \unit{cm^3/(g s^2)}, c = 3 × 10^{10} \unit{cm/s}. Definitions used in part (a): M is the stellar mass with M = 2 × 10^{33} \unit{g}, R is the stellar radius, rho is the mean mass density, m is the photon’s effective mass defined by E = m c^2, E is the photon energy, V is the gravitational potential energy at the surface, nu is the photon frequency at emission, and nu' is the photon frequency received at a large distance. Let R_crit denote the critical radius below which light cannot escape, and let rho_min denote the corresponding minimum mean density. Definitions used in part (b): Consider a homogeneous universe modeled as a uniform sphere of density rho(t). Let A and B be two comoving gas particles with separation R(t). The enclosed mass is M = (4/3) pi R^3 rho, which is constant during the expansion. Overdots denote time derivatives with respect to t. T and V are the kinetic and potential energies per unit mass, and K = T + V is the total specific energy. Neglect pressure forces. Let t_0 and R_0 denote, respectively, a reference time and the corresponding separation R(t_0) = R_0 used for integration constants.

subject: physics
problem: Question: An astronaut takes an ordinary flashlight, turns it on, and leaves it out in deep space, spin-stabilized about its axis so the beam direction is fixed. Assume the bulb is at the focus of a paraboloid reflector so essentially all optical power is emitted in one direction. Neglect external forces and assume the recoil speed remains nonrelativistic. Define the variables used in the solution: N is the optical power output of the flashlight in watts, t is the time interval the flashlight is on, m is the mass of the flashlight, c is the speed of light in vacuum, E is the total photon energy emitted over time t, p is the momentum delivered to the flashlight, v is the additional speed gained after time t, and \varepsilon is the energy of a single photon. For the numerical example use N=1\unit{W}, m=0.3\unit{kg}, and t=2\unit{h}.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A hypothetical flashlight emits a perfectly collimated beam of light along a straight line and can convert a significant fraction of its own rest mass into radiation. The flashlight starts at rest in an inertial frame with rest mass m0. After it is switched on, it recoils and moves freely along the same line as the beam due to conservation of momentum. At some instant its speed relative to the original rest frame is v and its rest mass at that instant is m. The speed of light is denoted by c. Let E be the total energy carried away by all photons emitted up to that instant; the total photon momentum has magnitude E/c and is directed opposite to the flashlight's velocity. Define beta = v/c and gamma = 1/sqrt(1 - beta^2). Do not assume v is much smaller than c. Find m as a function of v.

subject: physics
problem: Question: A particle with charge q and mass m moves in a circular orbit of radius R in the xy-plane in a uniform magnetic field B_vec = B \hat z. The laboratory inertial frame is S with Cartesian coordinates (x, y, z) and time t. The particle position is (x(t), y(t), z(t)). Define the 3-velocity vector v = (\frac{dx}{dt}, \frac{dy}{dt}, \frac{dz}{dt}) and the speed u = |v|. The relativistic Lorentz factor is \gamma_u = 1 \big/ \sqrt{1 - (u/c)^2}, where c is the speed of light. The relativistic momentum is p = m \gamma_u v. The motion has angular frequency \omega about the z-axis, with u = R \omega. Define the complex coordinate \xi = x + i y. Integration constants are \rho > 0, \varphi, and \xi_0 = x_0 + i y_0, where x_0 and y_0 are constants. Let \tau be the proper time of the particle. A second inertial frame S' moves with uniform velocity parameter \beta along +x relative to S (|\beta|<1), with Lorentz factor \gamma = 1 \big/ \sqrt{1 - \beta^2}. The 4-velocity is u^\alpha = (\gamma_u c, \gamma_u v_x, \gamma_u v_y, \gamma_u v_z), and its zero component measured in S' is u_0'. The 4-momentum is p^\alpha = (m u_0, p). The particle energy measured in any frame is denoted by \mathcal{E} and satisfies \mathcal{E} = c p_0 in that frame. In S the electric field is E_vec = 0 and the magnetic field is B_vec = B \hat z. For field transformations relative to a boost along +x, let the boost velocity vector of S' relative to S be w = \beta c \hat x. Components parallel and perpendicular to w are denoted by subscripts \parallel and \perp.

subject: physics
problem: Question: Two proton beams collide head-on, each proton having kinetic energy T. Find the kinetic energy K_single of a single proton beam that, when colliding with stationary target protons, yields the same available energy for reactions. Use relativistic expressions. Definitions: m is the proton rest mass, c is the speed of light, S' is the rest frame of a target proton in the single-beam case, E' and p' are respectively the total energy and momentum of the incoming proton in S', and K_single is the kinetic energy of that incoming proton. The available energy for reactions in the fixed-target case is defined by K_single = E' - m c^2.
answer: K_{single} = \frac{2 T^2 + 4 T m c^2}{m c^2}

subject: physics
problem: Question: A photon with laboratory-frame momentum magnitude p impinges on a particle at rest with rest mass m. Let c denote the speed of light in vacuum with SI unit \unit{m/s}. Let E and P denote the total energy and the magnitude of the total momentum of the system in the laboratory frame before the collision, respectively. Define E' and P' as the total energy and the magnitude of the total momentum of the system in the center-of-mass (COM) frame. The COM frame is the inertial frame in which the total momentum vanishes. Let beta and gamma be the Lorentz parameters of the boost from the laboratory frame to the COM frame along the initial photon direction, with gamma = 1/sqrt(1 - beta^2). In the COM frame, let p' denote the magnitude of the particle's momentum; the particle's momentum vector is opposite to the photon's momentum vector. In part (c), for elastic backward scattering in the laboratory frame, the final photon momentum vector is taken to be of magnitude p_1 opposite to its initial direction, and the particle's final momentum magnitude is p_2 along the initial photon direction.
answer: p_1 = \frac{p m c}{2 p + m c}

subject: physics
problem: Question: We consider the photoproduction reaction gamma + p -> p + psi' in the laboratory frame. The target proton is initially at rest. The incident photon has energy E in the laboratory frame. The mass of the psi' is taken to be M_psi = 4 M_p, where M_p is the proton mass. The speed of light is c. The threshold photon energy is denoted by E_0. For reference, M_p c^2 = 938\unit{MeV}. In what follows, E_tot and P_tot denote the total energy and the magnitude of the total momentum of the initial system in the laboratory frame, respectively. The laboratory speed of the produced psi' is denoted by v, and results may be expressed as v/c.

subject: physics
problem: Question: An antiproton of total energy E_0 collides with a proton at rest in the laboratory frame. The proton and antiproton have the same rest mass m. In the interaction, two identical particles are produced, each with rest mass m_x. One of the produced particles (label it particle 1) is detected at an angle of 90^\circ to the incident beam direction. Let E_s denote the total energy of particle 1. Let p_0 be the magnitude of the incident antiproton momentum, and let p_1 and p_2 be the magnitudes of the momenta of particle 1 and the other produced particle (particle 2), respectively. Let \theta be the angle between the momentum of particle 2 and the incident beam direction. The speed of light is c. Calculate E_s and show that it is independent of m_x and E_0.

problem: Question: A charged particle with charge e and rest mass m moves in a uniform electromagnetic field that is constant in space and time. In an inertial Lorentz frame S, the field components are E = (a, 0, 0) and B = (0, 0, b), with |E| ≠ |B|. Let c denote the speed of light and τ the particle's proper time. Let s be defined by ds = c dτ. Let u^α be the particle's four-velocity with components u_1, u_2, u_3, u_4, defined by u^α = ((γ/c) u, γ), where u = (u_x, u_y, u_z) is the three-velocity and γ = 1 / sqrt(1 - u^2 / c^2) with u^2 = u_x^2 + u_y^2 + u_z^2. Let p^α = m c^2 u^α be the four-momentum. Let F be the Lorentz three-force and F^α the four-force, with F = e (E + u × B) and F^α = (γ F, (γ/c) u · F). Use the Euclidean norm for |E| and |B|. Tasks: 1) State the differential equations for the components of the four-velocity u^α(τ). 2) Show that the solutions may be expressed as superpositions of exponentials and determine the exponents. 3) Determine the condition on E and B under which all components of the four-velocity remain bounded along every trajectory

problem: Question: A particle of charge e, rest mass m, and total energy E has velocity vector u and moves in the magnetic field generated by a magnetic dipole of moment vector M located at the origin and directed along the +z-axis. Use spherical coordinates (r, theta, varphi) with unit vectors i_r, i_theta, and i_varphi. The electromagnetic scalar potential is Phi and the vector potential is A. The vacuum permeability is mu_0 and the speed of light is c. The Lorentz factor is gamma. Initially the particle is in the xy-plane at radius r = R, moving radially outward so that at t = 0 we have r = R, theta = pi/2, dot r > 0, and dot varphi = 0. Let v denote the initial speed, i.e., v is the magnitude of u at t = 0. Assume the magnetic force confines the motion to the xy-plane and the orbit is bounded. Let p_varphi = partial L / partial dot varphi denote the conjugate momentum to varphi. Define alpha = (mu_0 / 4 pi) e M / (m gamma v). Let r_min and r_max denote the minimum and maximum radii attained by the particle. Find r_min and r_max.

problem: Question: It is well known that, to Newtonian order, planets move in elliptical orbits around the Sun. When only special-relativistic kinematics is included (keeping the Newtonian gravitational potential), the bound orbit becomes a precessing ellipse of the form $$\frac{1}{r}=\frac{1}{r_0}\{1+\epsilon\cos[\alpha(\theta-\theta_0)]\}$$ where r is the radial coordinate from the Sun, theta is the polar angle in the orbital plane, r_0 is a length scale, epsilon is a dimensionless eccentricity parameter, theta_0 is a phase constant, and alpha is a dimensionless parameter with alpha=1 corresponding to the classical result of zero precession. Throughout, let m be the planet mass, M the mass of the Sun, v the planet speed, c the speed of light, and G the gravitational constant. Define the Lorentz factor and dimensionless speed by $$\gamma=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\beta^2}}$$ $$\beta=\frac{v}{c}$$ In plane polar coordinates (r,theta), the velocity components satisfy $$v^2=\dot r^2+r^2\dot\theta^2$$ The conserved total energy is denoted by E and the conserved relativistic angular momentum magnitude about the Sun is denoted by b. These will be related to r_0 and alpha in part (a). All quantities are to be interpreted in SI units, and units in equations are written using \unit{ }.

problem: Question: Derive the Hamiltonian for a relativistic charged particle in prescribed electromagnetic fields (Gaussian units). Definitions and notation: - Rest mass: m_0 - Charge: q - Speed of light: c - Lorentz factor: gamma = 1 / sqrt(1 - v^2 / c^2) - Position coordinates: x_i for i = 1, 2, 3 - Velocities: dot x_i = dx_i / dt, collected as the velocity vector v = (dot x_1, dot x_2, dot x_3) - Scalar potential: Phi(x,t) - Vector potential: A(x,t) with components A_i - Electric field E and magnetic field H are defined by the potentials as below - The mechanical (kinetic) momentum is defined as p_mech = m_0 v / sqrt(1 - v^2 / c^2) - The canonical momentum components are defined by p_i = partial L / partial dot x_i, and p denotes the canonical momentum vector with components p_i - The Hamiltonian is denoted by cal H, and the Lagrangian by cal L. The electromagnetic fields are given by the potentials via
answer: \mathcal H = \sqrt{ \left( p - \frac{q}{c} A \right)^2 c^2 + m_0^2 c^4 } + q \Phi

problem: Question: A particle of rest mass m_0 moves at speed v relative to an inertial frame. Let c denote the speed of light in vacuum with units \unit{m/s}. Let E be the total relativistic energy of the particle, T its kinetic energy, and let the Lorentz factor be defined by \gamma = 1 \big/ \sqrt{1 - v^2/c^2}. If the kinetic energy equals the rest energy m_0 c^2, find v in terms of c.

problem: Question: A beam of electrons is scattered by a fixed target as shown in Fig. 3.27. The electrons are elastically scattered. Each electron has total energy E = (5/3) m0 c^2 and the beam has a flux of Q electrons per second. The incidence angle is 45° relative to the target normal, and the scattering is specular so that the angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence. Variable definitions and units: E is the total relativistic energy per electron in \unit{J}; m0 is the electron rest mass in \unit{kg}; c is the speed of light in \unit{m/s}; v is the electron speed in \unit{m/s}; \gamma is the Lorentz factor; Q is the number of incident electrons per second in \unit{s^{-1}}; pn is the component of an electron's relativistic momentum along the target normal before the collision in \unit{kg m/s}; \Delta p is the change in that normal momentum for one electron due to the collision in \unit{kg m/s} and is directed into the target; F is the average force on the target in \unit{N}. For elastic, specular scattering from a fixed target, the speed is unchanged and the normal momentum component reverses sign while the tangential component is unchanged.

problem: Question: Consider a very high energy scattering experiment involving two particles with the same rest mass m_0. In the laboratory frame, one particle is initially at rest and the other is incident with 3-momentum magnitude p and total energy E. Let c denote the speed of light. The center-of-mass (COM) frame is the frame in which the total 3-momentum is zero. Define beta^* = v^*/c as the speed parameter of the COM relative to the laboratory frame, where v^* is the COM speed. Let E^* denote the total energy of the two-particle system in the COM frame. Let \bar{E} denote the energy of each particle in the COM frame.

problem: Question: A particle of rest mass m has initial velocity v_0 along the x-axis at t = 0. For t > 0, it is subject to a constant force of magnitude F directed along the positive y-axis. Let the position be r(t) = (x(t), y(t)) and define the velocity components by v_x(t) = dx/dt and v_y(t) = dy/dt. Let the speed be v(t) = sqrt(v_x(t)^2 + v_y(t)^2). Let c be the speed of light. Define the Lorentz factor by gamma = 1 / sqrt(1 - v(t)^2 / c^2) and define beta = v(t) / c. Also define gamma_0 = 1 / sqrt(1 - v_0^2 / c^2). Find v_x(t), v_y(t), and v(t), and show that lim_{t -> infinity} v(t) = c.
